Categories:
- basic_research: Understanding mechanisms, pathways, disease processes (knowledge output)
- biotools: Developing research tools, assays, methods, platforms (tool output)
- therapeutics: Developing drugs, treatments, therapies (treatment output)
- diagnostics: Developing clinical tests, biomarker panels (diagnostic test output)
- medical_device: Developing physical devices for patients (device output)
- digital_health: Patient-facing software, apps, clinical decision support
- other: Health services, behavioral interventions, epidemiology

application_id: 11067411
activity_code: F31
title: Targeting the Regulation of High Endothelial Venules in HNSCC Regional Recurrence
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Narrative Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC) exhibits a proclivity for regional recurrence in the tumor draining lymph nodes (tDLNs) well after clearance of the primary tumor. This pattern of spread is highlighted by decreased lymphocyte trafficking and de-differentiated Lymphotoxin Beta receptor (LtBR) expressing High Endothelial Venules (HEVs) within the tDLN. We aim to decipher how regional recurrence is promoted in the absence of LtBR signaling, and whether LtBR agonism restores HEV functionality to mitigate regional recurrence.

application_id: 11169026
activity_code: K08
title: The Effect of Sodium (Na+) on Kidney B cells in Lupus Nephritis
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Lupus nephritis, an autoimmune kidney disease characterized by a T and B lymphocyte infiltrate, is associated
with significant morbidity and mortality yet no targeted treatments exist. This proposal will investigate the function
of kidney-infiltrating B lymphocytes and determine how B lymphocyte survival and function are influenced by the
high sodium concentrations found in the kidney tissue environment. The work will utilize both animal models
and human kidney biopsies and will help identify kidney-specific targets for the treatment of lupus nephritis.

application_id: 11088167
activity_code: K01
title: The Role of AMPK in Regulating Muscle Mass and Function in Cancer Cachexia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cancer cachexia (CC) is a wasting syndrome characterized by anorexia, weight loss, and muscle and fat loss,
which can be caused by cancer itself and often worsened by chemotherapy; current interventions for preventing
or treating CC are suboptimal. 5’-adenosine monophosphate-activated protein kinase (AMPK), an intracellular
energy sensor, plays a central role in skeletal muscle physiology by regulating mitochondria and it also shows
anti-inflammatory effects; however, the function of AMPK in CC and its potential therapeutic role in preventing
muscle wasting and weakness is unknown. The proposed study will characterize the role of AMPK in cancer-
and chemotherapy-induced muscle wasting and dysfunction in vivo and in vitro and evaluate the potential for
promoting AMPK activ...

activity_code: K08
title: Defining the Role of Palliative care for Patients with Hematologic Malignancies Undergoing Adoptive Cellular Therapy
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adoptive cellular therapy (ACT) is a complex, novel, and potentially life-saving therapy for patients with hematologic malignancies but is associated with significant toxicities, high symptom burden, and extreme prognostic uncertainty which results in poor end of life (EOL) outcomes. This clinical research and career development proposal will support an early stage investigator to refine and test a palliative care (PC) intervention (PEACE) tailored to the needs of patients receiving ACT and their caregivers given their profound unmet supportive care needs. The intervention aims to improve the quality of life, symptoms, coping, prognostic understanding, and EOL communication of patients receiving ACT and their caregivers and will lay the foundation to demonstrate the role ...

application_id: 10807705
activity_code: K08
title: Addressing the Vaping Epidemic in Adolescents and Young Adults: Advancing our Understanding of Cessation Treatment and Engagement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Approximately 14% of adolescents (As) and 10% of young adults (YAs), aged 18-24, use electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) and are at risk for health harms from toxicant/carcinogen exposure and long-term nicotine addiction. Evidence on how to help A/YA ENDS users quit is scant with only one randomized controlled trial which used the texting program, This is Quitting. The proposed work will identify strategies used by A/YAs to quit ENDS, probe for strategies to increase cessation engagement, and also test the feasibility in YAs of adding both financial incentives and nicotine replacement therapy to the existing cessation program, This is Quitting.

application_id: 11023037
activity_code: F31
title: Identification and structural basis of a potential licensing factor of the Integrator cleavage module
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative Currently, there is a gap in understanding how transcription is utilized to aid in DNA repair and maintain genomic integrity. The Integrator complex is known to associate with RNA Polymerase II and utilize its RNA endonuclease activity to regulate transcription at nearly all RNAPII loci. My research has uncovered a surprising interaction between a critical DNA repair protein and Integrator, and this proposal will elucidate the mechanistic underpinnings between these two important nuclear processes.

application_id: 10807843
activity_code: K08
title: Improving CAR T-cell therapy for Pediatric B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (B-ALL)
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Immunotherapy with T cells expressing so called chimeric antigen receptors (CARs) that target CD19 (CD19- CAR T cells) has been very effective for pediatric patients with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL). However, only approximately half of patients benefit from this approach long term. In this proposal, we aim to overcome current limitations of CD19-CAR T-cell therapy by generating these CAR T-cells from healthy donors and enhancing their anti-leukemia activity by additional genetic modifications.

phr: Project Narrative The University of Notre Dame proposes to create a Post-baccalaureate Research Education Program (ND-PREP) to provide a one year pre-PhD biomedical research experience for individuals from underserved diverse backgrounds. The goal of ND-PREP is to provide Fellows with the research experience, professional development, and life skills necessary to gain admission to and succeed in competitive biomedical PhD programs. This goal will be achieved by providing the Fellows with a three-part formal training program that includes: 1) an intensive in-laboratory research experience (75% of trainee time); (2) a set of skills-development workshops and enrichment activities that will also serve to build a strong ND-PREP cohort (15% of Fellow time, with workshops ranging from research et...

title: CTSA K12 Program at the University of Alabama at Birmingham
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There is an urgent need to improve health care delivery and reduce health care disparities in our geographic region. We will train and mentor the next generation of translational researchers and scientists with a primary focus on health outcomes and disparities among diseases disproportionately represented within an area of the southeastern US known as the “Deep South”.

application_id: 10807043
activity_code: K23
title: Machine Learning for CCHD Screening using Dynamic Data
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Congenital heart disease is the most common birth defect and can be missed during pregnancy scan and newborn exam leading to delayed detection, poor outcomes, and death. This application seeks to test and develop a critical congenital heart disease screening machine learning algorithm that combines the current screening standard, oxygen saturation, with additional features to assess blood flow and perfusion and incorporates dynamic data from both inpatient and outpatient settings. This approach has the potential to increase infant survival and will develop neonatal specific devices using artificial intelligence driven diagnostics, which are priorities to the NICHD Pregnancy and Perinatology Branch and Obstetric and Pediatric Pharmacology and Therapeutics Branch.

cancer, compared to the general population and to date relatively little is known about the safety of standard

comorbid conditions and may be under the care of multiple different specialists. The central focus of this

activity_code: K01
title: Dysregulation of Naïve T cell Quiescence during Aging
org_name: ALLEN INSTITUTE
phr: Project Narrative The breakdown of naïve T cell homeostasis is closely associated with increased susceptibility to infection during aging. However, very few strategies exist to study the mechanisms of long-term T cell maintenance in humans and particularly with age. The goal of this project is to utilize new single cell technologies to elucidate naïve T cell complexity and to develop novel methodologies to study age-related homeostatic decline, which will ultimately enhance immune protection against infections in older individuals.

application_id: 11023089
activity_code: F30
title: Leveraging Causal Inference and Machine Learning Methods to Advance Evidence-Based Maternal Care and Improve Newborn Health Outcomes
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In light of unsettlingly high rates of adverse newborn outcomes – such as perinatal mortality, low birthweight, and preterm birth – in the United States, evidence-based approaches to improve maternity care are vital. Medicaid provider incentives for provision of immediate postpartum long-acting reversible contraception and antepartum fetal surveillance offer two potential strategies to enhance outcomes via improvements in birth spacing and early detection of fetal distress, respectively. In the absence of a robust evidence base, the proposed research will rigorously estimate the impact of each strategy on newborn outcomes to inform data- driven clinical practice around postpartum contraception and antepartum surveillance.

application_id: 11023025
activity_code: K23
title: Genetic modifiers of Sickle Cell Kidney Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TENNESSEE HEALTH SCI CTR
phr: Project Narrative African Americans with sickle cell disease (SCD) and co-existing kidney disease have an increased risk of mortality. Recent genetic studies have shown a causal association of apolipoprotein L1 gene (APOL1) polymorphisms with markedly increased risk for development of kidney disease in African Americans. The proposed research aims to 1) elucidate the association between APOL1 as well as other selected genetic modifiers in the development and progression of chronic kidney disease in individuals with SCD and 2) to generate a validated multi-gene genetic risk profile to identify individuals at increased risk of chronic kidney disease which will set the stage for future clinical studies evaluating novel disease-modifying therapies.

activity_code: K08
title: Coordinated functions of ER quality control mechanisms in pancreatic islet α cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Pancreatic islet α cell dysfunction is one of the principal mechanisms underlying the hyperglycemia and impaired response to hypoglycemia in diabetes mellitus. The molecular mechanisms underlying the ability of α cells to maintain synthesis of the counterregulatory hormone glucagon are poorly understood. This study will delineate the communication between two key protein degradative mechanisms, namely endoplasmic reticulum (ER)- associated degradation (ERAD) and macroautophagy, in promoting ER protein quality control and α cell function.

application_id: 11138746
activity_code: K99
title: Novel Diurnal Model for In Vitro Fertilization Studies
org_name: MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The majority of modern women are exposed to reduced light quality from extended time spent indoors,
employment requiring nighttime work, and nighttime technology use, leading to numerous health risks,
including reproductive deficits. This basic science proposal will validate a diurnal rodent model to elucidate
both individual and combined contributions of photoperiod- and light intensity-driven adaptations in the female
reproductive axis. This work will determine the impact of light quality on trace element and neuroendocrine
mechanisms modulating fertility and in vitro fertilization success.

application_id: 10808062
activity_code: F30
title: The impact of proliferating mast cells in the developing brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Project Narrative Imbalances in the relationship between neuronal and immune cells during development cause inflammatory cascades which drive neurodevelopmental disorders, including autism and schizophrenia. This proposal seeks to understand how an unexpected immune cell, the mast cell, is recruited to the developing brain and promotes the normal developmental trajectory of surrounding glia. Elucidating the mechanisms by which peripheral immune cells shape neurodevelopmental processes will strengthen our understanding of how inflammatory disturbances cause neurologic disease.

application_id: 11290812
activity_code: K23
title: Time course and predictors of progression to postpartum prediabetes and type 2 diabetes in HIV: a prospective longitudinal study of low-income Indian women
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
HIV and gestational diabetes both increase the risk of developing type 2 diabetes postpartum,
but it is not known whether HIV adds to the risks brought on by gestational diabetes. I propose a
longitudinal cohort study following pregnant women with gestational diabetes with and without
HIV over 2 years postpartum to 1) determine how HIV affects the development of type 2
diabetes or its precursor, pre-diabetes and 2) characterize the longitudinal effect of HIV on beta
cell function, which is a major contributor to the pathophysiology of type 2 diabetes. The results
of this study will be used to develop lifestyle or pharmacologic interventions to prevent diabetes
in South Asian women with and without HIV.

phr: Narrative
Despite expected exponential increases in Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and related dementias, and an
overabundance of research indicating that preoperative cognitive impairment is a major risk factor for negative
post-operative outcome, we know very little of AD cognitive, biomarker, and anesthesia/surgical interactions and
have little to no evidence-based perioperative medical care strategies for AD patients. Via the K07 mechanism
and institutional support, the applicant will leverage novel perioperative clinical and state-of-the-art institutional
facilities to develop an institutional wide research and training program addressing these problems. The
proposed program will result in improved awareness of cognition and memory status in older adults prior to
surgical procedures with an...

application_id: 11178738
activity_code: K99
title: Functional Convergence at the Host-Virus Interface
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project narrative
The objective of this project is to prepare Dr. Ian Boys to transition to an independent position as a tenure-track
investigator. This project focuses on 1) using structural homology screens to identify and classify antiviral proteins
in biodiverse genomes, as well as anti-immune genes in diverse viruses, and 2) the study of protein families that
have diverged to serve distinct functions, with some of them evolving to become antiviral proteins. This
multidisciplinary evolution-driven study of the biology of infection presents an opportunity to study functional
convergence, with implications for our understanding of how proteins evolve to acquire novel function.

activity_code: F31
title: Defining the role of CD4 T cells in autoimmune CD8 T cell differentiation and function in type 1 diabetes
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project will characterize b cell-specific CD4 T cell populations and investigate their role in driving
autoimmune CD8 T cell stemness, differentiation, and function in type 1 diabetes (T1D). The proposed studies
will provide novel insights into T1D pathogenesis and identify therapeutic targets for the prevention and treatment
of T1D and other T cell-mediated diseases.

application_id: 11159464
activity_code: R61
title:  Mindfulness to Enhance Cognitive Health in Latino Older Adults at Risk for Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) is an incurable neurodegenerative condition that causes immense human suffering and economic burden that may be especially pernicious for Latino older adults. The proposed research will examine the feasibility and effectiveness of a new mindfulness training program designed specifically for this community, in terms of its ability to reduce the risk for AD by enhancing cognitive health and psychological well-being. This project directly addresses a major public health prerogative and holds high impact potential to yield valuable new tools for mitigating the risk of AD in communities that may be particularly vulnerable.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Implementation science (ImS) is a branch of research that focuses on the “use of strategies to adopt and integrate evidence-based health interventions and change practice patterns within specific settings” and is a major initiative of the NIH research portfolio. activity_code: F32
title: Analysis of oxidative stress through deep atomic embeddings
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project aims to predict the impact of oxidative stress on human proteins via a novel deep learning methodology. The deep learning approach involves encoding atomic protein geometry using a graph transformer-based autoencoder, enabling the biochemical information contained within known protein structures to be applied to tasks with limited training examples. Through this project, we will uncover functional insights into aging-related ailments, identify potential drug targets, and validate the computational findings through collaboration with an experimental lab.

activity_code: F32
title: How does myelin plasticity influence neural circuit dynamics required for long-term memories?
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The research project described in this proposal seeks to understand how myelin plasticity impacts neural
circuit dynamics during long-term memory recall. Using cutting edge approaches, I will identify the neural
populations that become myelinated after an aversive experience, and how the formation of new myelin
impacts synchronization across a cortical-hippocampal-amygdala network during long-term memory recall.
These findings provide insight into how non-neuronal cells can impact neural circuit dynamics to support
long-term memory recall.

activity_code: F31
title: Systems genomics approach to targeting glucose toxicity during pathological cardiomyocyte hypoxia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative Cardiac hypoxia is a prominent contributor to the pathophysiology of a myriad of cardiac diseases, constituting the leading causes of morbidity and mortality in the world. Cardiomyocytes have an innate ability to adapt to mild hypoxia through fuel switching over chronic timescales, however, the molecular mediators of this transition have not been fully elucidated. The goal of this proposal is to gain a comprehensive understanding of the pathways that facilitate adaptive and maladaptive metabolic rewiring (particularly for fuel use) in hypoxic CMs, with the long-term goal of targeting these pathways as novel therapeutics.

title: Sensory neuron-skin interaction in health and disease
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  In the skin layer, pain-sensing neurons and keratinocytes together detect and modulate painful stimuli, yet how their interaction is disrupted in neuropathic pain conditions is poorly understood. This proposal aims to characterize their interaction, regulatory mechanisms, and functional impact in disease by developing and using novel human models and simple in vivo animal models of chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy. activity_code: K23
title: Neural Mechanisms Underlying Self-Critical Rumination and Self-Reassurance and Suicidal Thoughts and Behavior in Youth
org_name: EMMA PENDLETON BRADLEY HOSPITAL
phr: Given that suicide is the second leading cause of death of children and adolescents, this study addresses the National Institute of Mental Health's call for greater understanding of the brain/behavior mechanisms underlying youth suicide. Specifically, we will use functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) and real- world ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to advance what is known about the brain/behavior mechanisms underlying self-critical rumination (defined as persistent negative self-evaluation) and self- reassurance (providing compassion to self) in the context of youth suicide risk. application_id: 11128360
activity_code: T90
title: Comprehensive Training Program in Oral Biology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: There is a critical need for a diverse group of highly trained basic and clinical oral health researchers. If
effective preventive therapies and cures for the many diseases associated with the oral, dental and craniofacial
complex are to be discovered and applied, a cadre of oral health researchers must be trained to work in
interdisciplinary teams and to maintain the highest standards for ethical and responsible conduct of rigorous
scientific research. The main objective of this comprehensive training program is to produce oral health
researchers who can apply state-of-the-art technologies to generate new knowledge, translate that information
to applied therapies, and rigorously evaluate the outcomes of implementation of novel preventive or treatment

title: Cortical-hypothalamic control of the cardiovascular consequences of stress
org_name: COLORADO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Cardiovascular diseases are the leading cause of death globally and chronic stress is a well-known risk factor for cardiovascular pathologies. title: Mind-Body Peer Support for Family Caregivers to Patients with Severe Acute Brain Injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Family caregivers to patients with severe acute brain injury (SABI) are at risk for chronic emotional distress, yet no tailored, efficacious interventions exist to promote their mental and physical wellbeing. The proposed study aims to lay the foundation for the development of a mind-body peer support intervention for SABI caregivers through conducting preliminary quantitative and qualitative work, and adapting existing interventions with stakeholder-engaged methods. Future work can evaluate the feasibility and efficacy of the proposed intervention, and if successful, the proposed mind-body peer support intervention has wide-reaching potential to be implemented in critical care settings and improve the long-term wellbeing of SABI caregivers.

title: Transcriptional control of T cell function during type 1 diabetes
org_name: NATIONAL JEWISH HEALTH
phr: NARRATIVE  T cells responding to tumors or chronic infection tend to become hyporesponsive, yet T cells retain function despite persistent antigen during autoimmune disease. application_id: 10808464
activity_code: K08
title: Epithelial-immune interactions in neonates with Spontaneous Intestinal Perforation
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative In the United States, about half a million infants are born prematurely, prior to 37 weeks gestation, annually. Extremely premature infants born before 28 weeks gestation with a birth weight <1000g are most susceptible to complications of preterm birth, including spontaneous intestinal perforation, where little is known about the pathogenesis of this disease, and there are no disease-specific therapies. The proposed research will elucidate how aberrant T-cell activation exacerbates epithelial cell damage in spontaneous intestinal perforation with the hope of providing avenues for future studies into therapeutic targets in at-risk neonates.

activity_code: K23
title: Early Detection of Gestational Diabetes Mellitus in Pregnancy
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: Project Narrative Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is one of the most common complications of pregnancy and is associated with significant negative consequences for the maternal-offspring dyads. Professional societies recommend screening for GDM; however, guidance is inconsistent and late in pregnancy, thus precluding meaningful time to intervene for maternal-offspring benefit. Early approaches to GDM screening relying on clinical biomarkers in the first trimester exist, and this study will create a protocol for implementation of enhanced first trimester GDM screening and perform a feasibility and acceptability trial in the first trimester among high-risk pregnant women.

application_id: 11032728
activity_code: F32
title: Striatal Dopamine's Role in Associative Learning and Behavior
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative
Impaired striatal dopamine signaling is linked to cognitive deficits in a variety of neuropsychiatric disorders. How-
ever, we have a limited understanding of how dopamine influences neural activity in the striatum to guide cogni-
tive processing. We will study how dopamine influences striatal activity during associative-learning tasks in ro-
dents, providing key direction for treating cognitive deficits in neuropsychiatric disease.

activity_code: F31
title: Plastic Chemicals in Pediatric Cardiopulmonary Bypass: Evaluating Cardiac Toxicity and Investigating Clinical Alternatives
org_name: CHILDREN'S RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: Project Narrative: Phthalate plasticizer exposure is prolific in clinical settings, with toxicity reported in many organ systems including the heart. Neonates and infants undergoing cardiopulmonary bypass have increased exposure to phthalate plasticizers through multiple medical interventions. This project will investigate the direct effects of phthalate plasticizers on human cardiac physiology, while also assessing whether alternative biomaterials or mitigation strategies can decrease phthalate exposure and improve health outcomes.

application_id: 11142307
activity_code: F31
title: Development of novel bacteriophage-based therapies to combat Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Candida spp. dual-species biofilms.
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The opportunistic pathogens, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Candida spp., are growing concerns in healthcare
settings due to their increasing rates of antimicrobial resistance, and are commonly co-isolated from infections.
The present study aims to develop two novel treatment strategies against such coinfections, ones using
antimicrobial peptides and bacteriophages, viruses that selectively infect bacteria. Not only would such
treatments provide alternative approaches to combating bacterial and fungal coinfections, but their development
would provide a new pipeline for treatment of additional polymicrobial infections and site-specific delivery of drug
compounds.

activity_code: K24
title: Mentoring translational scientists in international infectious disease research
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative Becoming a successful translational scientist in the field of international infectious diseases is challenging, requiring extensive training in leading field studies, developing and performing laboratory assays, and synthesizing and interpreting the data generated. This K24 renewal will provide Dr. Greenhouse protected time for mentoring and career development, allowing him to help train the next generation of scientists in this area, drawing upon his broad expertise and established research program. He will focus on leadership development, expand the scope of his research portfolio beyond malaria, and establish an interactive, online training platform in genomic epidemiologic analysis.

activity_code: K01
title: Within-person dynamics of cognition and personality in healthy aging and Alzheimer's disease.
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The research and training in this application will provide additional training and experience applying advanced intensive longitudinal statistical techniques to the study of aging and dementia. Outcomes in aging and Alzheimer disease (AD) research rely critically on the measurement of cognitive change and the innovative approaches described herein will provide novel and more sensitive analyses of longitudinal change. activity_code: F30
title: Inheritance and Reprogramming of Maternal Chromatin during Zebrafish Maternal Zygotic Transition.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative
After fertilization, zygotic genome activation is a critical process that enables the transition from maternal
program to zygotic program. Better understanding of the mechanisms underlying zygotic genome activation
has important implications for fertility treatments and maternal-fetal health. This proposal will investigate how
maternally provided DNA and proteins may regulate the accurate timing of zygotic genome activation.

application_id: 11119232
activity_code: R01
title: A Large-scale Efficacy Trial of a Resilience Training Program for First Responders
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY NEW YORK STONY BROOK
phr: Project Narrative.
The proposed research is relevant to public health by providing a large-scale efficacy test of the Worker
Resilience Training (WRT) program – a novel resilience building workshop for first responders. This test will
provide critical information necessary to understand the mechanisms of the WRT for preserving mental health
outcomes over the course of twelve months. Overall, the current proposal will strengthen the evidence base for
utilizing the WRT program to prepare and protect first responders from the effects of traumatic exposures.

application_id: 11245095
activity_code: F31
title: Neuroimmune mechanisms of coronavirus-induced lung inflammation and airway dysfunction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The recent COVID-19 pandemic has underscored a critical need to comprehensively understand how viral
infection promotes respiratory disease. This proposal will define the contribution of lung-innervating sensory
neurons to airway inflammation and respiratory dysfunction from coronavirus infection. These studies will
deepen our understanding of host-pathogen interactions and lay the groundwork for identifying new therapeutic
targets for respiratory disease.

activity_code: K99
title: Development of Highly Multiplexed Raman Imaging Platform for Alzheimer’s Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Immune cells have recently been recognized as vital players in Alzheimer’s disease, as they change with disease progression and can shape the disease pathology through complex mechanisms. We require a new imaging tool to closely study various immune cell populations simultaneously within the spatial context of human brain tissue. The complete molecular mapping of the brain-immune relationship, enabled by our Raman imaging approach, will pave the way for improving our ability to understand, prevent, and treat Alzheimer’s disease.

application_id: 11143724
activity_code: K08
title: Enhancing antigen presentation in triple negative breast cancers through CD40 agonist, Flt3 ligand, and anthracycline chemotherapy
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Breast cancers are associated with defects in antigen presenting cells, partially explaining poor
immune responses against breast cancers and limited benefit from current immunotherapies. We
recently identified that the novel combination of CD40 agonist, Flt3 ligand, and pegylated
liposomal doxorubicin improves antigen presentation and significantly controls tumor growth of
triple negative breast cancers in mice. This proposal will test this novel therapy in patients with
metastatic triple negative breast cancer and through a combination of patient tissue profiling and
mouse experiments will identify how the therapy works, which patients are likely to respond, and
mechanisms of treatment resistance.

application_id: 11366151
activity_code: F31
title: Metatranslatomics enables functional profiling of microbial competition in the Salmonella-perturbed gut
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
The bacterial pathogen Salmonella Typhimurium must outcompete members of the microbiome to establish
infection and cause disease. By understanding the mechanisms by which Salmonella (1) outcompetes specific
members of the microbiome and (2) manipulates host immune responses to aid in this process, this work will
uncover novel strategies to tip the scales of competition against Salmonella.

activity_code: F31
title: Splice variants of microtubule-associated protein 4 regulate the efficiency of organelle transport in dendrites
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
The dendrites of neurons atrophy during neurodegenerative diseases, which contributes to patients’ symptoms.
A link has been found between an aberrant increase in a protein called MAP4 and Alzheimer’s disease. The
work proposed here on the role of MAP4 in regulating the organization of structural elements within dendrites
may provide a mechanistic explanation for the dendritic atrophy found in Alzheimer’s disease.

application_id: 10825070
activity_code: F32
title: Longitudinal trajectories of white matter and reading development in bilingual children
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Experience-induced patterns of brain development in bilingual children have either not been taken into account or been explicitly excluded from foundational longitudinal neuroimaging studies characterizing developmental trajectories of brain language and reading networks. This creates critical barriers to understanding the development of reading networks in emergent typical and struggling bilingual readers. The overarching goals of this proposal are to disentangle how bilingualism and reading development shape brain structure during childhood and to reveal compensatory neural mechanisms associated with better outcomes in struggling bilingual readers.

application_id: 11173816
activity_code: K23
title: The relationship between HIV exposure, contact networks, and TB in children under 5 years old
org_name: BOSTON MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Tuberculosis (TB) is a top-ten killer of children worldwide and is viciously synergistic with the global HIV
pandemic. The projects proposed here will use unique databases and novel social network and ethnographic
approaches to quantify and describe the relationship between HIV, TB, and children’s social environments.
The results will inform child TB case detection strategies and policy to close the pediatric TB detection and
treatment gaps in high HIV/TB-burden settings.

application_id: 11162365
activity_code: K01
title: Defining the neural basis for persistent obesity
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: Project Narrative
While obesity affects more than a third of American adults, we still do not understand how obesity changes the
brain to drive feeding and further weight gain. The research proposed here is relevant to public health because
it will study neural circuits that reinforce feeding and reveal how they are strengthened in obesity. This aligns
with NIDDK’s mission to support research and research training focused on metabolic diseases including
obesity, and will ultimately lead to more targeted therapies to prevent weight gain.

application_id: 10844518
activity_code: K23
title: Evaluation of Angiopoietins as Prognostic Markers of Kidney Allograft Structure and Function
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Non-functioning kidney grafts after deceased-donor kidney transplantation remain a significant problem, with about one thousand grafts failing yearly and only 50% remain functional after ten years. The current tools we have to predict graft failure are limited in providing pathways for future intervention, therefore this project aims to identify if recipient protein markers involved in blood vessel stability are associated with future graft function in an effort to identify pathways amenable to intervention. The mission of this project aligns well with the overall mission of the NIDDK, as we aim to understand pathways that influence recipient graft outcomes to be able to enhance graft quality and improve overall recipient health.

title: Deciphering the role of phospholipid homeostasis in physiology and disease
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dysfunctional lipid accumulation is characteristic of numerous metabolic diseases and pathologies including
insulin resistance, heart failure, and hepatic steatosis. Enhanced understanding of the molecular mechanisms
controlling all aspects of lipid metabolism, such as phospholipid metabolism, holds great promise for the
treatment of these diseases. This proposal seeks to understand the importance of FLVCR1 mediated

title: Epigenetic Regulation of Microglia Dopamine System Interactions
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The development of the dopamine system is a tightly regulated process that has great implications on social and reward associated behaviors. title: Investigating the Impacts of Pharmacological Slow Wave Sleep Enhancement on Cognition and Memory Traces in an Alzheimers Disease Model
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a debilitating and irreversible neurodegenerative disease associated with major disruptions to sleep, an essential and ubiquitous process in all animals. Recent works reveal there is a strong bidirectional relationship between loss of slow-wave sleep (SWS), the deepest sleep stage, and the pathological and cognitive features of AD. Here, I will investigate whether chronic pharmacological slow-wave activity (SWA) enhancement can improve pathology and disturbances to sleep-wake rhythms and sleep architecture (Aim 1), as well as rescue behavioral and biological measures of memory deficits (Aim 2) in a mouse model of AD.

application_id: 11362960
activity_code: R25
title: Training in Research Undergraduate Experience through the Rutgers Addiction Research Center: The TRUE RARC Scholar Program
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The present lack of diversity among addiction researchers presents a range of opportunity costs, from hindered
scientific innovation to persistent disparities in treatment outcomes. The Training in Research Undergraduate
Experience through the Rutgers Addiction Research Center (TRUE RARC) Scholar Program serves to upskill
and diversify the next generation of addiction scientists at the undergraduate level via interdisciplinary
coursework, lab internships, and career readiness activities to promote a more diverse addiction research field.

application_id: 11065440
activity_code: F30
title: Genetic determinants of endoplasmic reticulum stress in OI and its consequences on cellular receptors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
OI patients experience brittle bone and recurrent fractures due to mutations impacting type I collagen
production, modification, or trafficking. Recent work has identified that misfolded type I collagen
accumulates in the ER and causes ER stress in certain individuals with OI -- it is not known if this
phenomenon is common to all forms of OI or if the process interferes with bone active therapeutics
used to treat OI. This proposal will establish that ER stress as a clinically important, genotype
dependent feature of OI and establish preclinical data for a new precision strategy to treat OI.

application_id: 10842259
activity_code: K08
title: A role for glycolipids and unconventional T cell subsets in alpha-gal allergy
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
Alpha-gal syndrome is an allergic condition with global reach; there are reports from around the world
describing these patients with delayed allergic reactions to mammalian meat, and yet, we have limited
understanding of the mechanisms underlying this condition. The proposed research will explore the role of
glycolipids and unconventional T cells in this allergy with the vision of broadening our understanding of their
roles in hypersensitivity disorders. It may also link tick exposure to a gene expression and cytokine profile in
unconventional T cells that promotes allergic responses.

application_id: 10837855
activity_code: K08
title: Characterizing Hepatitis B Transmission in Congolese Children
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hepatitis B (HBV) is a leading infectious killer in sub-Saharan Africa, despite the existence of an effective
vaccine. Children are especially vulnerable to HBV-related complications, but our knowledge of patterns of
childhood HBV transmission in African children are incomplete. In this career development proposal, Dr.
Thompson will apply comprehensive epidemiological, spatial and modeling analyses to enhance our
knowledge of childhood HBV in Africa, to identify hotspots of disease, and to model spatially targeted
vaccination strategies to avert future cases of HBV in African children.

application_id: 10980532
activity_code: K23
title: Midlife Vascular Risk Factors for Alzheimer's Disease in Persons with HFpEF
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mid-life cardiovascular risk factors contribute to the development of Alzheimer’s disease in later life and to Alzheimer’s disease progression. This study will examine the relationship between cardiovascular risk factors and Alzheimer’s disease biomarkers and cognitive function in middle-aged adults with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. Findings from this study will lead to the identification of pathways that could potentially be modified by interventions with the goal of decreasing AD risk in this high morbidity population.

application_id: 11251819
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of ADAM9 in the Innate Immune Response to SARS-CoV-2 Infection
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
During infection with severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SCoV2), a dysfunctional immune
response causes severe disease and acute lung injury (ALI), but this process is poorly understood. We
demonstrated that the protein ADAM9 is an important regulator of the immune response to viruses like SCoV2.
The goal of this study is to investigate ADAM9’s role in SCoV2-mediated immune dysfunction and the impact
on disease severity and development of ALI, which is crucial to developing novel therapeutics targeting
inflammation for disease modulation.

application_id: 10990267
activity_code: F99
title: Microbiome-Mitochondrial Interactions Driving Cancer-Induced Cachexia in the Obese State
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA AT COLUMBIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer-cachexia is a wasting syndrome that occurs in up to 80% of cancer patients and is the primary cause of death for 20-40% of cancer patients. While obesity has been suggested to protect against the severity of cachexia due to having ‘more to spare’, published work from my F31 demonstrates that obesity is not protective against cachexia and suggests exacerbated impairments to mitochondrial function and quality control with a particular disruption in the removal of damaged mitochondria. The goal of my F99/K00 is to 1) understand the microbial and metabolic perturbations with cancer cachexia in the obese state (F99), and 2) establish microbiome-mitochondrial interactions in obesity associated cancer cachexia and identify strategies for intervention (K00).

application_id: 11132574
activity_code: F31
title: Characterizing the role of confirmation bias in financial exploitation of older adults
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: Project Narrative
Confirmation bias is a ubiquitous phenomenon that can lead to a variety of negative outcomes, including financial
exploitation. Age-related differences in the function of the prefrontal cortex, coupled with deficits in cognitive
processes like working memory and error detection, may leave older adults particularly susceptible to this bias
and its harmful effects. Using computational modeling, noninvasive brain stimulation, and longitudinal methods,
we aim to establish a clearer connection between confirmation bias and mild cognitive impairment that will
provide insight into ways to mitigate its negative effects and protect older adults from fraud.

application_id: 10827914
activity_code: F32
title: Quantitative dissection of the events that encode bone size and shape during regeneration
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Unlike mammals, zebrafish possess the ability to regenerate entire appendages following traumatic injury, such as amputation, and these regenerated appendages faithfully recapitulate the size and shape of the original appendage. How size and shape are encoded during appendage regeneration in unclear, in part because there is an incomplete dynamic and quantitative understanding of the cellular events that underlie regeneration. This proposal will quantitatively define the dynamic molecular and cellular events that promote zebrafish appendage regeneration and uncover molecular events that are potential therapeutic targets for enhancing appendage regeneration in humans.

activity_code: F32
title: Bringing the clinic to the lab: the effects of forced and non-forced rehabilitation on functional recovery after spinal cord injury
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Sensory-based rehabilitation following spinal cord injury (SCI) facilitates neural rewiring for functional recovery, but it is unknown if different sensory-based training programs, such as forced exercise therapy and non-forced exercise, give rise to different rewiring strategies and behavioral outcomes. The proposed research will characterize the neural rewiring strategies that occur in forced and non-forced exercise and elucidate contributions of paradoxical training paradigms to motor performance and recovery. With a unique combination of techniques and expertise of my mentors, my research will break down current barriers of translational preclinical research with the aim of instructing rehabilitation programs for SCI patients with the intent to maximize recovery.

application_id: 10808795
activity_code: K01
title: Developing a smartphone-based intervention targeting high-risk situations of nicotine vaping among young adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Interventions to reduce e-cigarette use (nicotine vaping) among young adults is urgently needed given high prevalence of vaping in this age group, emerging vaping-related harms, and increased risk of tobacco smoking and other substance use escalation. This study innovatively capitalizes on cutting-edge methods of mobile health research to develop and pilot test a smartphone-based intervention delivering messages tailored to risky moments for vaping occurrence. This study will lay the groundwork for evidence-based innovative interventions to curb the vaping epidemic and prevent related harms among young people.

application_id: 11171628
activity_code: K23
title: Cellular and Metabolic Dysfunction in Sepsis-Induced Immune Paralysis
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Sepsis is a leading cause of mortality and critical illness worldwide, with recognition that it may lead to a profound
and sustained immunosuppression known as immune paralysis (IP). There is a critical need to understand the
underlying pathophysiology of sepsis-induced IP, with evidence now emerging that fatty acids (FA) and their
metabolites are important immune signaling molecules and regulators of cytokine release. The goal of this
proposal is to characterize metabolic and protein biomarkers of sepsis-induced IP by quantifying FA metabolites
in circulating leukocytes and evaluating the role of immune paralyzed response proteins in the plasma of septic
IP patients, thus identifying modifiable pathways that may be targeted to improve clinical outcomes.

application_id: 11249723
activity_code: K23
title: Investigating Inhibitory Control Networks in Parkinson's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative: Cognitive impairment in Parkinson’s disease (PD) is extremely prevalent, affecting more than
80% of patients over the course of their disease, yet few treatment options exist for these disabling symptoms.
Dysfunction in prefrontal cortex and subcortical areas likely contributes to PD cognitive impairment, however,
very little is known about the activity of and connectivity of these two brain regions. Here, we propose to
investigate cognitive networks in PD and to deliver low-frequency deep brain stimulation (DBS), a stimulation
pattern with promising early results on cognition, while assessing the effects on a cognitively demanding
inhibitory control task and prefrontal-subcortical electrophysiology.

application_id: 11026403
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of platelet stimulation in hemolysis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: NARRATIVE High-mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) has a recognized role in platelet activation and thrombosis formation, but its mechanism of platelet activation in sickle cell disease remains unclear. I propose that circulating hemoglobin oxidizes HMGB1 to trigger an intracellular platelet signaling cascade that potentiates platelet mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (mtROS) and platelet activation. Investigating this novel mechanism of platelet activation will improve our understanding of the mechanisms linking hemolysis and inflammation to platelet activation and thrombosis, and establish a foundation for developing and applying HMGB1-inhibitors to prevent thrombotic complications in sickle cell disease.

application_id: 11042696
activity_code: F31
title: Evaluating the impact of antigen selection on T cell responses to therapeutic cancer vaccination
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative Cancer vaccines are an emerging class of cancer immunotherapies that can stimulate immune responses that specifically target cancer cells. Unlike preventative vaccines for infectious diseases, the immune system in cancer is exposed to the vaccine target prior to vaccination and in some cases mounts responses against the cancer prior to vaccination. This project aims to utilize mouse models of cancer to study how the presence or absence of an anti-cancer immune response prior to vaccine therapy impacts vaccine responses and efficacy, as well as how to predict whether a patient will have a relevant pre-vaccine immune response or not.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the sixth leading cause of death in the United States, affecting almost 6 million people which is expected to more than double by mid-century. The neuropathologic hallmarks of AD including β-amyloid (Aβ) plaques and tau neurofibrillary tangles accumulate in the brain decades before symptom onset, and drive downstream neurodegeneration, cognitive impairment, and death. Identifying genetic drivers of early neuropathologic changes like conversion to Aβ positivity (Aβ+) may lead to novel therapeutic targets useful earlier in the disease cascade when treatment is most effective.

title: Improving Risk-Stratification in Patients with Prolonged ICU Stays
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Prolonged intensive care unit (ICU) stays are devastating, expensive, psychologically and ethically challenging for providers, patients and families, and disproportionally burdensome to healthcare systems. Traditional risk- prediction models have failed to identify patients at risk of prolonged ICU stays early in their ICU course, hindering advancements in the implementation of evidenced-based practices to prevent its development. activity_code: K08
title: Targeting CNS Neuroinflammation in Traumatic Brain Injury by Nasal Anti-CD3
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In traumatic brain injury (TBI), persistent neuroinflammation contributes to disease progression and poor outcome, and microglia plays a critical role in the neuroinflammatory response following injury. We have discovered a mechanism to decrease the inflammatory nature of microglia, so they are more protective rather than disease-inducing by the nasal administration of anti-CD3 that stimulates immune cells to travel to the brain and decreases inflammation. This work seeks to test this treatment in animal models of TBI to determine its effect on the brain and immune system over time and potentially generate novel therapeutic options for the millions of Americans with TBI.

title: Dissecting neuroinflammatory mechanisms in a model of progressive TDP-43 pathology
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative 97% of ALS patients and 57% of Alzheimer’s Disease patients present with dysregulation of the protein TDP-43, causing disruption of a variety of critical genes, leading to neurodegenerative disease. Neuroinflammation, an understudied facet of ALS and Alzheimer’s Disease, occurs when overactive immune cells cause catastrophic damage to otherwise healthy neuronal tissue. Using neuron/immune cell culture and a mouse model of TDP-43 pathology that exhibits a progressive neurodegenerative phenotype, this project aims to understand the genetic and molecular regulators of neuroinflammation and characterize potential therapeutic targets for ALS and Alzheimer’s Disease.

application_id: 11113588
activity_code: K08
title: The Phex Pathway: Mechanisms Underlying a Meniere's Disease Subtype
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS EYE AND EAR INFIRMARY
phr: Project Narrative
 Meniere’s disease causes disabling symptoms of fluctuating and progressive hearing loss and episodic
vertigo, but little is known about its etiology. In this grant, we aim to improve our understanding of the etiologic
mechanisms leading to the development of a subtype of Meniere’s disease by studying the inner ear physiology
and morphology in humans and mice with X-linked hypophosphatemia. We hypothesize that the molecular
mechanisms leading to the development of endolymphatic hydrops and symptoms of auditory and vestibular
dysfunction in this small cohort may serve as the “Rosetta Stone” for delineating the etiological origins of
Meniere’s disease more broadly.

title: Endothelial Cell Cycle Control to Normalize the Tumor Vasculature
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The dysfunctional tumor vasculature worsens disease by preventing drug delivery and promoting metastasis and
immune evasion. I propose to identify mechanisms of tumor vascular dysfunction and determine how an FDA-
approved cell cycle inhibitor used to treat breast cancer affects tumor endothelial cells, which is currently not
known. We hypothesize that cell cycle inhibition will normalize the tumor vasculature, expanding its therapeutic
use to other cancer types and other prevalent diseases characterized by pathological angiogenesis.

title: Non-Invasive Imaging Biomarkers to Identify a High-Risk Chronic Kidney Disease Phenotype
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Reductions in kidney blood flow can lead to permanent damage to the kidneys, known as chronic kidney disease, which can progress to complete loss of kidney function and can shorten life span. We propose to investigate whether information about kidney blood flow that we will obtain from contrast enhanced ultrasound will help identify which individuals with chronic kidney disease are at high risk of experiencing progressive loss of kidney function. The high-risk individuals can then be included in future clinical trials to test new treatments for chronic kidney disease.

application_id: 11179457
activity_code: F30
title: Redefining the factors that determine tear film stability to develop novel therapeutics for evaporative dry eye disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative
Evaporative dry eye disease is a common ophthalmic condition that is caused by decreased stability of the tear
film, usually due to abnormalities in the tear film lipid composition and/or secretion. This proposal will investigate
the mechanisms that govern tear film stability and modulate them with a novel, targeted therapeutic nonpolar
lipid found in the tear film of the rabbit. Additionally, this proposal will investigate the effect of this targeted
therapeutic on tear film lipid composition and secretion and the effect of the rabbit lipid on ocular surface health
in an animal model of evaporative dry eye disease.

title: Predictive utility of polygenic risk scores for chronic kidney disease.
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chronic kidney disease (CKD) affects 10-15% of the population world-wide and is associated with high mortality. We propose to develop and validate polygenic risk scores for CKD and its subtypes to aid in the early detection of kidney disease, before its onset, providing orthogonal risk information to the traditional disease risk factors. This proposal will lay groundwork for potential use of polygenic predictors of kidney disease in a clinical setting.

application_id: 10885368
activity_code: K01
title: Exploring Advance Care Planning Among Older Adults Across Racial, Ethnic, and Cognitive Differences Using Data Science
org_name: CLEMSON UNIVERSITY
phr: Compared to non-Hispanic, white older adults, older Black and Hispanic adults experience higher age-adjusted rates of Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD), disparities in healthcare access and utilization, worse quality of care at the end of life, and lower rates of advance care planning. This project will address a significant gap in knowledge regarding the complex patterns and interactions of risk factors related to dispari- ties in end-of-life care across cognition levels and races/ethnicities. The goals of this career development award are to use advanced statistical and artificial intelligence methods to understand the factors related to advance care planning and end-of-life care quality among older adults living with ADRD and their caregivers.

application_id: 11030818
activity_code: K23
title: Non-invasive oscillometry to measure lung mechanics, response to treatments, and predict longer-term pulmonary outcomes among preterm infants: a prospective cohort study
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There are approximately 360,000 survivors of preterm birth per year in the US, and these infants have a higher risk of wheeze, asthma, and longer-term lung disease. Methods to assess disease severity and response to treatment have been limited by the difficulty of performing pulmonary function tests among infants. This project will use non-invasive oscillometry to measure lung mechanics among spontaneously breathing healthy term infants and preterm infants with and without lung disease, to improve understanding of developmental lung trajectories and determine the extent to which this novel tool can measure disease severity, quantify response to treatments, and predict longer-term lung health among preterm infants.

title: Program in Blood Coagulation and Vascular Biology
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Research in hemostasis, thrombosis, and vascular biology addresses fundamental mechanisms underlying bleeding, thrombotic disorders, and diseases of the blood vessels. This Program in Blood Coagulation and Vascular Biology will train the next generation of scientists to study problems related to hemostasis, thrombosis, and vascular biology.

application_id: 11250434
activity_code: K23
title: Changes in cognition and psychiatric disorder symptoms during cannabis abstinence using a novel discordant twin design
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Widespread changes in cannabis availability and societal acceptance highlights the importance of characterizing cognition and psychiatric disorder symptoms when regular cannabis users remain abstinent. Results from the proposed experimentally-manipulated discordant twin study will provide evidence to support or contradict a causal model regarding the effects of cannabis abstinence on cognition and psychiatric disorder symptoms. Depending on the results, this study could provide motivation for cannabis users to quit or cut back or help in the identification of individuals who are at-risk for worse cognitive outcomes.

application_id: 10827859
activity_code: F31
title: Novel spatial-motor approaches targeting post-stroke spatial neglect and gait deficits
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Spatial neglect is a common symptom after stroke and negatively impacts the ability to achieve independent functional mobility post stroke. This NRSA F31 proposal aims to explore and generate new information regarding the combination of novel motor and spatial training approaches (prism adaptation therapy + electrical stimulation and prism adaptation therapy + gait training) to enhance the rehabilitation of spatial neglect and walking function in individuals post-stroke. This work will generate novel information that will inform future design of new and targeted treatment protocols for evidence-based rehabilitation of spatial neglect and gait dysfunctions post- stroke, to improve gait function and quality of life in stroke survivors.

activity_code: F31
title: The Novel Role of Cilia in Astrocyte Sevelopment
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Astrocytes serve critical roles in the function of the brain, such that disruptions to astrocytes underlie several neurodevelopmental disorders, making it crucial to improve our understanding of the signals that direct astrocyte development. This proposal will investigate primary cilia, signaling antennae present in almost every cell with critical roles in development, to determine how they regulate different stages of astrocyte development. This work will inform novel functions of astrocyte cilia and advance our fundamental understanding of ciliary signaling in developing astrocytes.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: || PROJECT NARRATIVE Iron deficiency is the cause for over 50% of anemias and is associated with heart failure, chronic kidney disease, and chronic inflammation. application_id: 11218660
activity_code: F31
title: In vitro nonhuman primate model of frontotemporal dementia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) is an Alzheimer’s disease-related dementia that has been linked to mutations in
the gene encoding for the neuronal protein tau (MAPT). This project will study this disease in stem cell-derived
neurons from skin cells of patients and rhesus monkeys carrying the same naturally-occurring FTD-related
mutation (MAPT R406W). The direct comparison of human and monkey cells will allow us to validate the monkey
cells as an in vitro model of FTD, understand pathological mechanisms across primate species, identify disease
biomarkers, and ultimately, test potential new treatments before preclinical testing in the valuable monkeys for
studies aimed at human clinical trials.

application_id: 11000271
activity_code: F32
title: Probing D2 and 5HT-2A mechanisms in early visual processing in V1
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE This research will provide critical new knowledge to our understanding of the receptors underlying neuromodulatory influences on circuits in primary visual cortex. In particular, this research will determine if there are differences in magnocellular vs parvocellular pathways in early processing by dopamine D2 or serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, and if center-surround mechanisms as a function of stimulus size, orientation, or contrast are modulated by D2 or 5-HT2A. The outcomes of this research will strengthen our understanding of neuromodulatory mechanisms in visual processing, and provide key insights into why some schizophrenic patients develop changes in early visual processing and see improvements with antipsychotic medications.

application_id: 11167422
activity_code: F32
title: Convergence of top-down and bottom-up thalamic inputs in medial entorhinal cortex
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Patients with Temporal Lobe Epilepsy and Alzheimer’s Disease suffer from debilitating impairments of learning
and memory, which can have devastating consequences on everyday actions such as the ability to safely
navigate back home. While considerable work has identified how neurons in the medial entorhinal cortex and
hippocampus collectively form a “map” of the external environment to enable spatial learning and memory, little
is known about how these maps can be flexibly used across environments with different cognitive demands.
Here, we propose to use cutting-edge technology to determine how brain-wide interactions shape the flexibility
of neural activity in entorhinal cortex, which in turn, will aid in elucidating potential neural mechanisms which may
go awry in neurological disorders.

application_id: 11031290
activity_code: K23
title: Artificial Intelligence Analysis of Myopic Vitreoretinal Pathology
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Currently, no methods exist to prevent the onset of retinal tears and subsequent rhegmatogenous retinal detachments except to caution patients of new floaters, flashes of light and curtains in their vision. In spite of these warnings, patients may present late after the onset of symptoms resulting in poorer visual outcomes even after detachment repair. In this proposal, Dr. Cassie Ludwig aims to determine whether the use of clinical structured and unstructured variables in the electronic health record in addition to ultra-widefield color fundus photography can aid in improving prediction of risk of tears and detachments in myopic and non-myopic patients and thereby allow physicians to consider early prophylactic treatments.

activity_code: K01
title: Microvascular mechanisms underlying white matter lesions in older adults
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Beta-amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles are characteristic of Alzheimer’s disease (AD), but white matter lesions of presumed vascular origin are an independent contributor to cognitive decline in AD patients. application_id: 10826142
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of protein quality control in osmotic stress adaptation
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Hypertonic exposure causes cell shrinkage and is thought to induce in vivo protein misfolding and aggregation, which are hallmarks for aging, cancer, and neurodegeneration. However, no studies have determined which endogenous proteins are compromised by hypertonicity, and little is known about the mechanisms that maintain proteome integrity during osmotic stress. The experiments proposed here will characterize the protein quality consequences and responses to hypertonic exposure, identifying determining factors for protein aggregation, refolding, and degradation that may inspire therapeutic strategies for protein-misfolding diseases.

activity_code: F32
title: Synthetic Biology Approach in Engineered Algae for Improved Milk Fat Composition in Formula
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project Narrative Human milk fat, which provides over 50% of an infant’s energy requirement during early lactation, has an optimized composition with respect to fatty acid chain length and regiospecific distribution. The fat ingredient of infant formula, which is typically derived from vegetable oil blends, does not match the structural diversity of human milk lipids with respect to chemical composition, and hence lacks some significant nutritional benefits of human milk fat. This project aims to provide a healthy and sustainable lipid source for infant formula by engineering an oleaginous green alga for human milk fat production through a synthetic biology approach that exploits the vast knowledge base of plant and algal lipid metabolism.

activity_code: F30
title: Investigation of Chromatin Modifiers to Elucidate the Phenotypic Variability of Congenital Heart Disease in Patients with 22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: In 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, around 60% of patients present with cardiac defects, largely due to a deletion that includes TBX1; this provides a unique population in which to investigate possible genetic modifiers of heart development. Our lab performed whole genome sequence analysis on human subjects and discovered a subset of chromatin regulatory genes associated with heart defects in 22q11.2 deletion syndrome. This study aims to determine the mechanism by which Tbx1 interacts with these chromatin regulatory genes, particularly Kmt2c and Kmt2d, in mouse heart development.

application_id: 11143634
activity_code: F31
title: Cognition, Vascular Function, and Physical Activity in Colorectal Cancer Survivors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Colorectal cancer survivors (CRCS) are at an increased risk of negative physiological and psychological
consequences such as cognitive impairment and vascular dysfunction that might be worsened by physical
inactivity. The proposed study will yield a novel understanding of these variables in CRCS and also provide new
insights into the associations among these variables as well as providing understanding on the putative
mechanism of vascular function mediating the relationship between physical activity and cognition.

application_id: 11143659
activity_code: K08
title: Targeting an unrecognized checkpoint on T cell function in tumors
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Recent advances in cancer immunotherapy have irrefutably demonstrated the powerful ability of the immune
system to destroy cancer through the activity of T cells, however the benefits of these treatments have been
restricted to a small percentage of patients. We found that cancer cell death leads to an altered abundance of
potassium within tumors, which acts as a previously unidentified ionic check upon T cell antitumor function.
The proposed research will assess the functional consequences for cell death and local potassium release for
in vivo T cell antitumor function in human cancers, leverage our preliminary data to define a non-canonical role
for intracellular potassium (K+), and test novel cancer treatments based on the directed manipulation of T cell
potassium transport.

title: Personalized Therapies of Pediatric Sepsis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH

application_id: 11031302
activity_code: K23
title: The role of cross-system collaboration in implementation of evidence-based substance use practices in child welfare
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Parental substance use (SU) is a leading cause of recent increases in CWS cases, and parental SU is associated with worse outcomes; however, engaging parents in SU services is challenging, as it requires collaboration across multiple systems (e.g., CWS, SU treatment agencies) and there are multiple points where parents can fall out of care. There is a critical need to better identify gaps in service provision in order to inform selection of appropriate EBPs that meet the needs of this population. The proposed project seeks to utilize community engagement and data-driven processes in EBP adoption and implementation in order to improve SU service provision for parents involved in CWS.

activity_code: F32
title: A multi-modal approach for efficient, point-of-care screening of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The scope of screening programs for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, an often underrecognized cardiac condition associated with significant morbidity and mortality, is limited by the reliance on comprehensive imaging and genetic tests that are costly, time-consuming, and not widely available. title: Patient-Reported Benefits of Cochlear Implant Listening Configurations
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Currently, the decision to continue using bimodal hearing or pursue a second cochlear implant (i.e., transition
from bimodal hearing to bilateral cochlear implant use) is highly subjective, and no clinical guidelines exist to
support this decision. Assessing the patient-reported benefits of each listening configuration has been
understudied; however, previous literature and our preliminary data suggest that patients hold valuable insights
into real-world listening benefits that may differentiate bimodal hearing and bilateral cochlear implants. In this
study, we will investigate the patient-reported advantages and limitations of each listening configuration using a
novel research approach.

activity_code: F32
title: In vivo mechanisms of Mycobacterium tuberculosis control and susceptibility
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project Narrative This project seeks to identify novel positive and negative regulators of protection against Mycobacterium tuberculosis, the single deadliest pathogen in the world. Leveraging exciting preliminary data of Mtb-infected lungs analyzed by single cell RNA-sequencing, this project will identify pathways used by macrophages to restrict tuberculosis growth, as well as identify the mechanism by which neutrophils inhibit productive responses against this infection. application_id: 11031347
activity_code: K23
title: Prognostic modeling of pediatric tracheostomy-associated respiratory tract infections
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Little is known regarding why children with tracheostomy experience recurrent respiratory infections despite receiving clinically appropriate antibiotic therapy. This proposal seeks to derive a novel prognostic model that incorporates clinical factors and mechanistically-based laboratory measures of pathogen antibiotic resistance and host immune response to identify children at greatest risk for recurrent tracheostomy-associated respiratory infections (TARIs). The findings of this work under a career development award will provide the basis for future risk-stratified interventional trials employing targeted therapies to reduce the frequency of TARI hospitalization among children with tracheostomy.

application_id: 11143928
activity_code: K01
title: Targeting alpha-cell GPCRs to stimulate glucagon and counter hypoglycemia
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hypoglycemia is a dangerous condition resulting from exogenous insulin therapy in Type 1 Diabetes that could
be remedied by endogenous glucagon secretion. We found that while alanine or glucose-dependent
insulinotropic peptide (GIP) alone induce modest increases in glucagon secretion, the combination of alanine
and GIP synergistically increase glucagon secretion. In this proposal, we will test the hypothesis that -cell
secretion of glucagon is able to mitigate insulin-induced hypoglycemia.

application_id: 10590310
activity_code: R24
title: Peer Recovery Innovation Network (PRIN)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCIENCE CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Peer Recovery Innovation Network (PRIN) convenes and coordinates national peer recovery support service
community-based experts, substance use researchers, and individuals with lived opioid and other substance use
disorder experience. The overarching public health significance is to reduce OUD-related morbidity and mortality
by ensuring that PRSS programs provide maximum benefit for those recovering from SUD through the
implementation of rigorously tested, empirically-support PRSS approaches. This project will support these goals
and augment the existing Consortium on Addiction Recovery Science network with the infrastructure
development outlined in this project.

application_id: 10684937
activity_code: U54
title: Consortium of Eosinophilic Gastrointestinal Disease Researchers
org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: Project Narrative (Overall)
Eosinophilic esophagitis, eosinophilic gastritis, eosinophilic gastroenteritis, and eosinophilic colitis are
challenging rare diseases that make it difficult to live normally or impossible for many suffering individuals to eat
many or all foods. These diseases are relatively poorly understood compared with other forms of allergy and
gastrointestinal inflammatory diseases, in part due to their rare nature and early stages of research. Herein, we
propose to partner with researchers, patients, and other key groups to better understand, treat, educate, and
optimize clinical trial readiness of these rare diseases.

application_id: 10916259
activity_code: F31
title: Intracellular pH Dynamics in Zebrafish Cranial Neural Crest Development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Intracellular pH (pHi) dynamics are a critical regulator of numerous cell behaviors, including cell migration, cell adhesion remodeling, and cell fate specification, yet the role of pHi dynamics during embryonic development in vivo remains largely unknown. The objective of this proposal is to address this gap by determining the function of pHi dynamics in the development of cranial neural crest, a highly conserved multipotent embryonic cell population, using zebrafish as a model. My findings have promise to reveal new insight on the cellular and molecular factors controlling craniofacial development, with important implications for human congenital diseases and tissue repair.

application_id: 11031844
activity_code: K99
title: T cell surveillance of senescent cells under homeostasis and aging
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Age-associated changes in T cells compromise their functionality and render older adults vulnerable to infections and age-related diseases, yet it remains unclear how T cells and their age-related dysfunction contribute to senescent cell accumulation. The overarching goal of this project is to 1) investigate how senescent cells stimulate T cell-mediated surveillance under physiological conditions, and 2) explore how T cell aging facilitates senescent cell immune evasion resulting in pathological senescent cell accumulation with human aging and disease. Discoveries from these studies will contribute to the development of immunotherapies for the elimination of pathological senescent cells, thereby supporting healthy aging.
