application_id: 11019691
activity_code: F32
title: Leveraging a budding yeast model to characterize the functional consequences of mutations in histone genes that are implicated in human disease
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
While histone proteins are critical for packaging DNA and regulating gene expression within the cell nucleus,
small changes that occur due to missense mutations in genes encoding these histones can convert them into
disease-causing variants. The proposed investigations use two complementary approaches, combining rapid
discovery in budding yeast with extension of these findings into human cells. These studies allow us to use
disease-causing histone variants to unravel cellular pathways and functional connections fundamental to
maintaining proper gene expression and protecting genome integrity.

title: Training in Alcohol Research
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is a major public health concern both in terms of its impact on personal and family
relationships and its tremendous economic costs due to lost productivity and health-related impairments.

basis of addiction and the limitations of currently available intervention approaches. The proposed project will

title: Development and Neuroadaptations in Alcohol and Addictions (DNA2)
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NY,BINGHAMTON
phr: Alcohol use disorder and other addictions have their origins in early development; consequently, the next generation of addiction researchers should be trained to conduct rigorous work that uses developmental approaches to characterize antecedents leading to these conditions and strategies to mitigate these outcomes. The T32 training program in Development and Neuroadaptations in Alcohol and Addictions (DNA2) at Binghamton University addresses this critical gap by providing highly qualified pre- and post-doctoral trainees with an interdisciplinary training program that focuses on research into developmental influences on drug and alcohol use and use disorder from a translational perspective.

activity_code: F31
title: Determining the role of TP53 regulation in alveolar epithelial transitional cell state,  and the contribution of alveolar epithelial transitional cell state to pulmonary fibrosis
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  According to the World Health Organization, in 2019 three of the top ten causes of death were respiratory diseases, contributing to over 8 million deaths every year. The proposed research will investigate the molecular mechanisms underlying cell identity in the alveolar epithelium, which will provide crucial insights into how lung tissue properly repairs itself during regeneration, and how this process contributes to fibrosis when it goes awry. The results of these studies will provide key insights into the basic biology of molecular determinants of cellular identity, and open avenues for the development of therapeutics that can improve the lives of the millions of patients living with respiratory disease.

application_id: 10837059
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating type 1 diabetes pathogenesis using the live pancreas tissue slice platform
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative  Type 1 diabetes (T1D) is an autoimmune disease characterized by autoantibody development and beta cell dysfunction prior to the immune-mediated destruction of the insulin producing beta cells of the pancreas and clinical diagnosis. The goals of this proposal are to determine the roles immune dysregulation plays in beta cell dysfunction, the mechanistic origin of the beta cell dysfunction, and whether MHC class I hyperexpression develops and causes the beta cells to be more visible to the immune system. The information gained through this proposal will be used to determine the mechanistic origin of T1D and to identify possible therapeutic targets to halt disease progression.

application_id: 10813717
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanisms of Abnormal Cranial Mesenchyme Morphogenesis in the Hectd1 mutant
org_name: CHILDREN'S RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Neural tube defects are among the most common structural birth defects and can lead to long-term disability and death. These malformations result from a failure of the flat neural plate to roll into a neural tube during embryogenesis, a complex process involving morphogenesis of not only the neural plate but the underlying cranial mesenchyme. This project aims to elucidate the molecular mechanisms that drive cranial mesenchyme morphogenesis contributing to neural fold elevation.

application_id: 10916292
activity_code: F32
title: Individualized Profiles of Sensorineural Hearing Loss from Non-Invasive Biomarkers of Peripheral Pathology
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE People with similar degrees of sensorineural hearing loss, as defined by the audiogram, often have varying levels of difficulty understanding speech in noisy environments. To assess the relationship between dysfunction in the auditory periphery and perception, the proposed project uses coordinated non-invasive biomarkers sensitive to multiple sites of cochlear dysfunction in both humans and chinchillas with sensorineural hearing loss to estimate profiles of peripheral pathology and predict hearing outcomes in background noise. Results from this cross- species study will provide valuable insight into the dysfunctions that underlie common auditory complaints and improve personalization of treatments for sensorineural hearing loss.

application_id: 10846260
activity_code: DP2
title: Psychophysical distillation of pure pain by nociceptive-specific heating
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Quantitative sensory testing (QST) and brain neuroimaging can both give important mechanistic insight into
chronic pain, but they could be improved by a technique that can stimulate nociceptors in isolation. Our lab has
developed a procedure for nociceptive-specific heating (NoSH) stimulation with no interference from innocuous
warm fibers. Nociceptive sensitivity will be measured in patients with fibromyalgia, a nociplastic pain condition,
and compared with data from pain-free controls, which may constitute a novel biomarker for use in other
mechanistic studies and clinical trials.

application_id: 10836451
activity_code: T32
title: Translational Alcohol Research Program (TARP)
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Alcohol use disorders (AUD) and alcohol-related liver disease (ALD) are major public health problems with
substantial and yet rising levels of morbidity and mortality worldwide. The goal of the Translational Alcohol
Research Program (TARP) is to facilitate the ability of emerging post-doctoral researchers to bridge the gap in
the application of knowledge gained from basic to clinical and social behavioral science research in AUD and
ALD. The TARP is designed to improve clinical treatment of AUD and ALD by increasing the availability of
appropriately-trained young scientists who are conversant in basic and clinical sciences and able to comfortably
traverse the translational gap between these sciences.

activity_code: F30
title: Ionizable Lipid Nanoparticles for Fetal Lung Targeting
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Congenital lung diseases are a significant source of pediatric morbidity and mortality for which no definitive treatments exist, although next generation in utero gene therapy may be applied to these disorders to correct pathogenic mutations prior to birth. While animal studies of viral vector delivery of in utero gene therapies are encouraging, identification of alternative, potentially safer delivery approaches will significantly advance the field toward clinical translation. In this proposal, we investigate the potential of ionizable lipid nanoparticles for this application by generating novel mouse fetal lung optimized ionizable lipid nanoparticles.

title: Multidisciplinary Research Training in Gerontology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative
To improve the health of America’s aging society requires greater understanding of the complex
factors affecting cognitive and physical health during aging. Since aging health is affected by
biological, sociological, economic, and psychological factors which operate within a policy,
epidemiological, and environmental context, multidisciplinary training is essential for future
researchers in aging. This program provides developing researchers with the tools to
understand the complexity of age-related health outcomes and to enrich their work by placing it
in a larger context.

application_id: 11134699
activity_code: F99
title: Investigating novel gene regulatory networks for development of myeloid cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NOTRE DAME
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Constructing the brain requires the coordination of several different cell types that permanently reside within the central
nervous system. One of these cell types, microglia, is the resident immune cell of the CNS and must be produced during
embryogenesis to maintain brain homeostasis throughout life. We are studying gene regulatory networks governing
microglia production during embryogenesis to help identify novel genes that can expand the knowledge of genetic risk
factors and therapeutic targets for diseases where microglia are the cause.

activity_code: K00
title: Dissecting mechanisms of inflammation driven cancer susceptibility in epithelial barrier tissues
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative:  The body’s epithelial barriers are routinely subjected to inflammatory stressors, necessitating robust regenerative potential to sustain barrier integrity. Following recurrent exposures to inflammatory stressors, tissues and their stem cells become increasingly susceptible to cancer formation. The proposed research will evaluate the long term effects that inflammation has on barrier tissues and how these effects lead to epigenetic rewiring in stem cell populations that results in cancer susceptibility and tumor formation.

application_id: 10687672
activity_code: DP2
title: Multi-scale feedbacks for robust organ development
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The research proposal will provide critical insights for how organs with the right shape form
reproducibly during embryonic development, using an organ common to all vertebrate animals—
the inner ear, in a tractable animal model— zebrafish. The fundamental principles revealed from
this study will address: (1) what is the normal variability during embryonic development, (2) how
much variability developing tissues can tolerate; and 3) how is variability resolved by tissue
geometry and multi-scale feedbacks to robustly give rise to correct organ shape. The research
will improve our understanding of successful embryonic development and provide mechanisms
for developmental disorders to reveal underlying causes for birth defects.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
The major mission of this T32 is to train dedicated, motivated, and well-qualified predoctoral and postdoctoral
fellows in aging biology and translational gerontology research. Given the projected increase in the number of
older adults living in the US by 2050, it is critical that significant advances be made toward understanding the
biology of aging and related diseases, and that these findings translate into enhanced care of older patients. The
present T32 Program will provide state-of-the-art training in aging biology research to talented scientists,
preparing them for academic careers at institutions throughout the country and equipping them to make novel
contributions toward understanding the biology and enhancing management of disabling diseases of aging.

title: Identifying Mitochondrial Mechanisms Driving Learning and Memory
org_name: MAX PLANCK FLORIDA CORPORATION
phr: Project Narrative
We aim to discover novel mitochondrial mechanisms driving learning and memory. This research will significantly
impact human health as mitochondria are hotspots of dysregulation in neurodegenerative, neuropsychiatric, and
neurodevelopmental diseases. Novel insights from our findings will also help develop future diagnostic and
therapeutic strategies for many conditions associated with mitochondrial impairment, such as Alzheimer’s,
Parkinson’s, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.

application_id: 11031714
activity_code: K08
title: Immuno-Metabolic Regulation of Vascular Dysfunction by CD70
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cardiovascular diseases comprise the leading cause of death in the United States, but mechanisms leading to endothelial and vascular dysfunction are still poorly understood. The research outlined in this proposal will elucidate how the protein CD70 regulates endothelial metabolism and vascular function. The long-term goal of this Career Development Award is to provide a pathway for the PI to transition to an independent physician- scientist while uncovering how CD70 regulates key endothelial redox and metabolic processes to influence the development of vascular dysfunction, thereby gaining new insights into the potential of CD70 as a novel therapeutic target for vascular diseases.

title: Neurobehavior, Neuropathology, and Risk Factors in Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Alzheimer's disease (AD) and related dementias affect a growing number of people as the population ages. Currently, nearly 6 million persons in the US have AD; it is estimated that by the year 2050, that number will be 14 million. With this increase in the number of AD patients, there is a corresponding growth in the number of family members and other caregivers affected by this devastating disease, as well as dramatic social and economic costs. The goal of our research training program is to prepare a new generation of investigators to translate mechanistic scientific discoveries into effective tools for diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of AD and related dementias.

phr: NARRATIVE  Aging and diseases associated with age clearly result in dependence and a severe reduction in health-span; however, without a clear understanding of the interactions between aging and disease, effective therapies cannot be developed. application_id: 11144273
activity_code: F31
title: Intensive Longitudinal Associations between Stress, Mindfulness, and Alcohol Use in Highly Stressed Mothers
org_name: GEORGE MASON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
This study uses ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to investigate momentary associations between
stress and alcohol craving and use in a group of highly stressed mothers of adolescents and whether
mindfulness moderates this association. The goal of this study is to identify targets for interventions that may
disrupt associations between stress and drinking. Findings from this study will be used to inform interventions,
including smartphone-based just-in-time interventions, for alcohol-related problems that may be tailored to
meet the needs of women who have been underrepresented in alcohol literature and who have unique social
demands and stressors (e.g., parenting).

application_id: 10844800
activity_code: DP2
title: Regulation of inflammation by nociception
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: Project Narrative
Bidirectional crosstalk between sensory neurons and immune cells is central for integrating a wide range of
signals, promoting responses against noxious stimuli while preventing exacerbated responses against benign
stimuli. Dysregulation of this balance can lead to maladaptive responses culminating in chronic conditions
involving inflammation, pain and itch. This study seeks to probe neuro-immune interactions that promote tissue
homeostasis, which may result in novel therapies for chronic conditions involving pain, itch and inflammation.

title: Exploring the Role of DMBT1 Suppression in Invasion of Oral Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Oral squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) has a high recurrence rate that leads to poor patient survival. Tumor
progression and recurrence rely on invasion of tumor cells away from the main tumor, so investigating
mechanisms responsible for invasion are extremely important for identification of novel therapeutic targets that
could improve survival. The objective of this project is to understand how SCC co-opts adjacent ‘normal’
epithelium to promote invasion.

application_id: 11131002
activity_code: T32
title: Stroke and Alzheimers Disease Related Dementias
org_name: WEST VIRGINIA UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Public Health Relevance: Stroke - like many brain diseases - is clearly associated with aging and a plethora of
age-related conditions, but age-related co-morbidities, as a variable in stroke occurrence, severity and long-term
recovery is seldom studied experimentally, but is one of the identified factors in the lack of progress in the
discovery of new therapies for acute stroke brain damage. The Stroke and Alzheimer’s Disease Related
Dementias Predoctoral Training Program will formalize and standardize our already strong training programs in
the biomedical sciences and focus on aging and stroke research by creating a new generation of young scholars
who can address the need for innovative stroke research for the citizens of West Virginia and the nation.

application_id: 11161639
activity_code: K43
title: Adapting evidence-based stigma reduction interventions to address HIV and drug use stigma among family members of people who use drugs
org_name: MUHIMBILI UNIVERSITY/ ALLIED HLTH SCIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
HIV and drug use stigma are a barrier to HIV prevention, care and treatment services access among people
who use drugs. Although a family is critical to support people who use drugs to enter and remain in HIV and
drug prevention and treatment, globally there are no interventions to address HIV and drug use stigma that
people who use drugs face from their family members. This study will address this gap by adapting existing
evidence-based health facility HIV and drug use stigma reduction interventions and create a stigma-reduction
intervention for family members of people who use drugs that will address both HIV and drug use stigma
coming from family members going towards people who use drugs.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE GSDMs are associated with many human diseases, and deletion of GSDMD significantly decreases pyroptosis and remarkably protects mice from sepsis. title: The role of pleural mesothelium in lung injury, repair, and regeneration
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pleural mesothelial cells are often not included in the discussion of cells involved in lung injury and repair,
largely because their role has yet to be thoroughly investigated. Intriguingly, these cells appear to be activated
in both conditions. This project will study the involvement of pleural mesothelial cells in injury, repair, and
regeneration and will investigate their direct impact on specific cell types in the lung to determine their
contribution as pathogenic or reparative.

activity_code: F30
title: Mitochondrial quality control as a selective vulnerability in cancers with chromosome 10q deletion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer is a leading cause of death in the United States, and this study seeks to identify novel vulnerabilities of a class of particularly lethal tumors, including glioblastoma and prostate cancer. When cancers delete the tumor suppressor gene PTEN, they frequently co-delete a neighboring gene called ATAD1, which is important for mitochondrial protein quality control. We will investigate how loss of ATAD1 may make cancers more likely to activate intracellular signaling pathways that result in cell death, and how this trait could be targeted in cancer therapy.

activity_code: F32
title: Molecular impact of endolysosomal dysfunction on neuron-glia communication pathways
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Microglial neuroimmune interactions and retrograde trafficking via the retromer complex are critical cellular processes in Alzheimer’s disease (AD) pathobiology and dementia. Previous studies have focused on the effects of retromer risk genes in neurons, but emerging genetic evidence implicates microglia, the brain’s resident macrophages, in the pathogenesis of AD. This proposal aims to characterize the effects of retromer disruption on neuron-microglia crosstalk involved in neurodegeneration using mouse and stem cell-derived models combined with novel proteomics tools to identify endosomal signaling changes.

activity_code: F31
title: Paracrine signaling from keratinocytes establishes a pro-melanoma environment
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative In the normal epidermis, ultraviolet radiation exposure induces the protective tanning response which transiently upregulates keratinocyte to melanocyte crosstalk. Melanoma cells hijack the protective tanning response, forcing keratinocytes to release factors that enrich for or stimulate the emergence of stem-like properties in melanoma cells. This project will investigate the mechanism by which melanoma cells activate pro- melanomagenic signaling in keratinocytes and the extent to which that signaling enhances melanoma migration, invasion and drug resistance.

application_id: 11141809
activity_code: F31
title: Elucidation and improved control of human induced pluripotent stem cell cardiac differentiation by using single-guide RNA-based cellular barcoding to track and manipulate lineages
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Human induced pluripotent stem cells (hiPSCs) have tremendous potential in the development of preclinical heart models but are limited in their clinical relevance due to low, inconsistent, and heterogeneous cardiomyocyte (CM) yield after differentiation. The proposed work will investigate how different hiPSC clonal lineages respond to differentiation cues, characterize lineages based on affinity for becoming CMs, and target low-affinity lineages with additional differentiation cues to increase quantity, consistency, and homogeneity of CM yield. This will elucidate mechanisms affecting hiPSC cardiac differentiation and generate methods for producing hiPSC- derived CMs on a clinically relevant scale.

application_id: 11165313
activity_code: F31
title: Testing of the VTA - EC Ca2+ – BBB Hypothesis
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Increasingly, the Blood-Brain Barrier is viewed as a dynamic regulator of
pharmacological signaling between brain and body. Chiefly comprised of tightly coupled
Endothelial Cells (EC), the Blood-Brain Barrier’s permeability is modulated by EC calcium
signaling processes across timescales. Here, I systematically test the hypothesis that behavioral
salience and reward signaling evokes fast, local EC Ca2+ dynamics to modulate BBB
permeability in Somatosensory Neocortex on short timescales.

activity_code: F30
title: Optimizing Ice-free Cryopreservation for Efficient Banking of Fresh TMJ Disc Allografts
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Temporomandibular joint (TMJ) replacement surgeries, used for patients suffering from a wide range of TMJ disc degenerative conditions, are restricted by unsuitable implant materials. While fresh TMJ donor allografts hold promise as a viable option, their potential remains largely unexplored due to limited availability and inefficient preservation methods. The goal of the proposed study is to overcome the current limitations of TMJ replacement surgeries by developing and optimizing an ice-free cryopreservation method to preserve cells, tissue structure, and biomechanical properties of TMJ disc allografts during long-term storage.

title: Developing Patient-Centered Decision Support for Eosinophilic Esophagitis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) is an understudied chronic inflammatory disease with increasing prevalence, incidence, healthcare costs, and burdens to quality of life. As clinical equipoise exists between effective medication and dietary therapies, complex decision making around treatments and chronic disease management is challenging for patients and providers, and understanding the drivers of decision making and applying shared decision making are critical gaps in patient-centered EoE care. This proposal aims to leverage patient and provider experiences and preferences to develop and test a multilevel decision support intervention that will help patients with EoE and their physicians share information to support informed decision making in selecting treatments.

title: Investigating contributions of BRCA2 DNA-binding domains toward maintaining genome integrity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCIENCE CENTER

application_id: 11144519
activity_code: K08
title: Improving risk prediction for children seeking emergency medical services (EMS) care with non-emergent complaints
org_name: CHILDREN'S RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: NARRATIVE: The lack of validated pediatric risk prediction tools for emergency medical services (EMS) is a significant problem, resulting in under-triage (where children with urgent medical needs are left at the scene) and over-triage (where children with no urgent resource needs are taken by ambulance to an emergency department). This mentored career development award seeks to address this problem by identifying patient factors associated with under-triage, then refining and pilot testing the Pediatric Observation Priority Score (POPS) for use in EMS agencies. Validating a pediatric risk prediction tool for EMS use would enable EMS agencies to develop evidence-based and patient-centered alternative dispositions (including treatment in place) that are safe and efficient.

application_id: 10814394
activity_code: K23
title: The effect of gestational age at delivery on lactation outcomes in pump-dependent mothers of critically ill infants

application_id: 11113807
activity_code: T32
title: Predoctoral Training Program in Immunology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: The immune system provides humans with critically important protective responses to infectious pathogens
such as viruses and bacteria, as well as to tumor cells; but inappropriate immune responses can also cause
inflammation and autoimmune diseases. Studies that advance understanding of immune responses, and how
to apply this knowledge to preventing and treating human diseases have great potential to reduce the disease
burden and promote the health of the nation. The Predoctoral Training Program in Immunology is designed to
provide rigorous training in a supportive environment for graduate students to pursue multiple approaches to
understanding the mechanisms underlying immune responses, and apply this knowledge toward the treatment
of human disease.

title: Investigating the role of DNA structure in centromere specification
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Ectopic centromere formation on chromosomes is associated with developmental disorders and genome instability in cancer. The goal of these proposed studies is to determine how DNA structure plays a role in centromere formation at specific loci that lack conserved DNA sequence motifs to facilitate chromosome segregation. application_id: 11184171
activity_code: F31
title: The genomic and synaptic basis of language disorder and learned sound association
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: Project Narrative
Learned sound association is the process by which the auditory nervous system associates a sound
with a certain outcome, and this ability can be impaired in neurodevelopmental conditions such as
language disorder and autism spectrum disorder (ASD). In this proposal, I aim to identify variants and
genes affecting language disorder and study the neuronal pathways and circuits involved in sound
association along with the mutations that can disrupt them. This will be achieved by using
computational approaches to analyze large-scale human genetic data and conducting behavioral
experiments with fiber photometry in wildtype and mutant mice models using an interactive virtual
reality (VR) environment.

title: Structural and Funcional Vascular Phenotyping in Smokers with Pulmonary Hypertension using Artificial Intelligence
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Pulmonary hypertension (PH) is a heterogeneous high mortality condition arising as a primary condition as well as a complication of COPD, heart failure, and clots in the lung. In carrying out the research outlined in the following proposal, we plan to develop and apply computational methods based on deep learning to quantify the structural and functional changes that can be seen using CT scans in the lung of patients with PH arising as a comorbidly of COPD. The result of this work will help us develop non-invasive ways of detecting PH in smokers, improve our understanding of the disease, and potentially enable better, targeted therapies.

application_id: 11031995
activity_code: K23
title: Medicaid Accountable Care Organizations (ACO) and Quality of Care for Adults with Serious Mental Illness (SMI)
org_name: BOSTON MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE An increasing number of patients with serious mental illness receive care in Medicaid Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs), an alternative payment model in which global payments and quality benchmarks are used to incentivize quality and lower spending, but little evidence exists on quality of ACO care for this patient population. We propose to identify features of Medicaid ACOs (e.g. provider-led vs. hospital-led ACOs) that produce the highest quality ACO care for adults with serious mental illness. We will compare stability of enrollment, care quality, and patient experience between differing ACO types using mixed methods, including quantitative analysis of claims data and qualitative interviews with patients and case managers.

application_id: 11077314
activity_code: K01
title: Addressing Barriers to the Comprehensive Use of Statins in HIV: The ABACUS-HIV Study
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite the clinical benefits of antiretroviral therapy, persons with HIV remain at high risk of atherosclerotic
cardiovascular disease, which is a growing clinical and public health concern as the HIV population ages. This
study will adapt an intervention promoting statin adherence from the general population to persons with HIV and
evaluate the adapted intervention’s acceptability and feasibility by conducting a pilot in an HIV clinic. This grant
will produce preliminary data and a prototype intervention for future intervention studies aiming to improve
atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease prevention efforts for persons with HIV.

application_id: 11371345
activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Mitochondrial Acid-Sensing Ion Channel 1 in Pulmonary Hypertension
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEW MEXICO HEALTH SCIS CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Pulmonary hypertension (PH) due to chronic lung disease and/or conditions causing hypoxia is a major cause of mortality and morbidity in the United States. With these diseases, enhanced pulmonary arterial constriction and remodeling cause increased resistance that leads to right heart failure and mortality in humans. The proposed studies are relevant to the mission of the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute because they will mechanistically examine the novel role of a molecular chaperone in an ion channel signaling cascade, which is required for the development of PH, thereby providing potential therapeutic targets to enhance the quality of life and prevent death in patients with these diseases.

application_id: 11032023
activity_code: F31
title: Siglecs in the Porcine Oviduct: Roles in the Sperm Reservoir and Sperm Immune Response
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT URBANA-CHAMPAIGN
phr: Project Narrative  Cells lining the lower part of the oviduct retain sperm to form a reservoir before fertilization. The goal of this study is to define how complex sugars on the sperm surface and newly-discovered Siglec proteins in the reservoir surface contribute to sperm adhesion and regulation of the local immune system. The long-term outcome of our findings could result in the development of novel contraceptive targets and help us understand some of the causes of idiopathic infertility.

less than 20% of pregnant women with SCD in the Greater Accra Region. This mentored award is focused

org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: Project Narrative
Despite scientific advances in combating deadly infectious diseases and successes in treatment in developed
countries, the world-wide death toll continues to rise because new diseases continue to emerge, drug resistance
is increasing, and there are no effective vaccines for some of the deadliest diseases. To address these
challenges, the Graduate Program in Tropical Infectious Diseases (GPiTID) is embedded in a school of public
health but focuses on fundamental scientific discovery using cutting edge approaches of genomics, cell biology,
immunology, population biology and gene editing, coupled with epidemiology of the diseases in their natural
setting, to address major infections that pose a threat to public health both in the US and abroad. There is a

application_id: 10933828
activity_code: T32
title: Tri-Institutional Molecular Mycology and Pathogenesis Training Program
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE Fungi are a vast and biologically diverse group of organisms that affect all of our lives in myriad ways. Fungi are important causes of human and crop diseases, affecting literally billions of lives each year. Most of these devastating effects occur through the loss of life of infected and immunocompromised patients, as well as loss of crop yield throughout the world. Additionally, fungi are also important scientific models to better understand basic physiology of more complex organisms, such as humans. In this program, we are training the next generation of scientists to explore fungi in many areas of biological investigation, both as infectious agents as well as model systems to understand genetic disorders and evolution and plant-fungal symbiotic interactions.
terms: Institution;Molecular;Pathogenesis;The science of Mycology;Training Programs
---
application_id: 11100005
activity_code: T32
title: Vermont Immunology / Infectious Diseases Training Grant
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VERMONT & ST AGRIC COLLEGE
phr: Narrative
Infectious diseases remain a leading cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, and present an ever-
increasing threat to human health. The development of new, innovative strategies to combat infectious disease
will require research scientists with a depth and breadth of training in both microbiology and immunology, and
an appreciation for the needs and realities of clinical care. The purpose of this application is to continue and
improve upon a highly successful graduate training program that provides promising predoctoral scientists with
the interdisciplinary training and skills required for them to launch successful independent careers in infectious
disease research.
terms: <Communicable Diseases><Grant><Immunology><Infectious Diseases><Infectious Disorder><Training><Vermont>

application_id: 10861739
activity_code: T32
title: Infectious Disease Training in Clinical Investigation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
This T32 training program is designed to train new clinical, epidemiological, and translational
infectious disease investigators, who are needed to discover better ways to diagnose, treat, and
prevent serious emerging and re-emerging infections and other Global Health threats such as
HIV/AIDS, influenza, meningitis, and antimicrobial-resistant infections. The program draws on
multiple schools and colleges within the University of Minnesota, plus a Global Health course
and an international site, to provide post-doctoral trainees – primarily Infectious Disease
physicians – with the advanced skills needed for a successful career in infectious diseases
research.

phr: The major goal of this ongoing, successful training program is to provide microbiology trainees additional knowledge in infectious diseases and the skills to recognize opportunities for translation of new knowledge into clinical applications. An optimal environment is provided for (1) training in the latest concepts and techniques of microbiology research, (2) enhancing understanding of current challenges in infectious diseases, and (3) preparation for careers that influence research efforts that lead to more rapid resolution of important infectious disease problems. The program will continue to recruit highly qualified trainees, increasing the pool of scientists performing research at the cutting edge of the microbiology/infectious disease interface.

application_id: 11244105
activity_code: F32
title: Understanding how evolutionary forces shape genomic imprinting and affect healthy development
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: NARRATIVE
For most of our genes, we express the copies we receive from both parents, but some genes
are subject to genomic imprinting, a special type of intergenerational regulation of gene
expression that specifies if a gene is expressed from the copy inherited from the mother or the
father. Genomic imprinting is essential for healthy embryonic development, and mutations in this
process cause several diseases; however, little is known about the evolutionary forces that
shape imprinting. In this project, I will determine how positive and purifying selection shape
genetic variation in imprinted genes and how genetic variation can lead to imprinting
incompatibilities between parents.

title: Regulation of Lymphatic and Vascular Remodeling in Acute Kidney Injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) is a serious clinical problem that affects ~20% of hospitalized patients and 60% of critically ill patients while increasing risk of developing chronic kidney disease. The overall goal of this project is to understand cellular and molecular mechanisms that govern how lymphatic and blood endothelial cells in the kidney respond to injury and impact AKI pathogenesis. title: Stability of Odor Representations in the Olfactory Tubercle

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The olfactory tubercle (OT) integrates information from the olfactory bulb and ventral tegmental area,

activity_code: T32
title: Biodefense Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS MED BR GALVESTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Many microbes under study at UTMB are potential biological weapons as well as naturally emerging/re-emerging pathogens. To protect the world from intentional release of biological weapons, as well as from naturally emerging/re-emerging infectious diseases of potential pandemic nature, improved diagnostics, therapeutics, and vaccines are needed. Our training program capitalizes on UTMB’s unique high containment facilities, multiple highly qualified, world-renowned faculty, and robust biosafety educational programs to train doctoral students to become future successful independent researchers in academia and pharma working to develop novel medical countermeasures to curb highly consequential diseases.

application_id: 11230112
activity_code: K23
title: Quality of Communication with Parents of Critically Ill Infants
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative
Evidence suggests the long term impact of poor communication with parents of critically ill infants may be
lasting and negative. However, not enough is known about high quality communication with parents of critically
ill infants: what it is, how to measure it, and how it impacts parents. This proposal seeks to 1) define high
quality communication with parents of critically ill infants; 2) evaluate the psychometric properties and validity
of a measure of high-quality communication for parents of critically ill infants; and 3) explore the long-term
impact of communication in the ICU on parents of ICU patients.

title: The roles of AP-1 pathway activation in NK cell development and exhaustion programming in AML
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The studies outlined in this proposal aim to determine the mechanisms by which activating protein-1 (AP-1)
influences natural killer (NK) cell development and function in the setting of acute myeloid leukemia (AML). Our
comprehensive approach will allow for a deeper understanding of the developmental and functional

studies outlined in this proposal will reveal novel mechanisms that hinder NK cell development and function in

title: Lipid peroxidation- and pyroptosis-induced tissue factor activation in pathogen-induced blood coagulation
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Inappropriate blood coagulation during serious infection leads to the formation of blood clots that contribute to, pulmonary embolism and heart attack, leading to human suffering and death. This proposal seeks to understand how certain viral and bacterial infections converge on a pathway of programmed cell death called pyroptosis that leads to activation of blood clotting. Our data identify an enzyme in lipid metabolism central to this process that may be a new drug target to prevent inappropriate clotting during infection.

title: Investigating the regulation and mechanism of tension-sensors Stu2 & Ndc80c
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative Proper cell division requires proteins in the kinetochore complex to sense tension exerted on chromosomes to ensure they are segregated correctly. Although harmful genome imbalance known as aneuploidy results when this process goes awry, the regulation and molecular details of this tension-sensing activity remain unknown. In this proposal, we will investigate how cells regulate protein factors to control kinetochore tension-sensing and also how these proteins function to carry out these mechanisms, which will give insight into how aneuploidy arises and how to prevent it.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In this day and age, brain autopsy is still regarded as the gold standard and only a definite diagnostic tool for ADRDs however, available brain tissue in the US largely originates from non-Latino Whites. Despite persistent and continuous efforts, Latinos face well-documented barriers to agreeing to brain donation. The current Mentored Research Scientist Development Award (K01) project will provide the foundation for developing and implementing effective and culturally tailored educational instrument and material in an effort to increase brain donation intention to consent and participate among diverse older Latinos.

title: TGFβ Restricts Tfh2 Differentiation but Augments IgE-Mediated Mast Cell Function: Critical and Opposing Roles in Allergic Disease
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: TGFβ signaling plays a multifaceted role in immune cell regulation but its role in T follicular and
mast cell development and function is unclear and its role in the joint regulation of both cell
types is inadequately studied. We show that TGFβ signaling suppresses human and murine
Tfh2 development to restrain IgE sensitization while promoting mast cell IgE anaphylactic
reactions, demonstrating a divergent role of TGFβ in allergic diseases. This application will
expand our work and delineate a mechanism for these observations.

application_id: 11032773
activity_code: K23
title: Real-world Data Enables Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic-based Drug Dosing in Critically Ill Children
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Drug dosing in critically ill infants is typically based on drug disposition at a single time point, and does not account for substantial, time-varying physiologic alterations that affect drug disposition and drug response, or the reciprocal effects of drug response on disposition. This oversimplified approach to drug dosing results in therapeutic failures and toxicities, but traditional clinical pharmacology approaches do not generate the necessary data to efficiently address this problem. This proposal will use an innovative combination of high frequency real world data collected per routine ICU care and advanced pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic modeling techniques to develop a systematic approach to inform safe and effective drug dosing in critically ill children.

application_id: 11032351
activity_code: K01
title: Investigation of the Molecular Mechanisms Underlying Astrocytic CREB's Regulation of Cocaine Drug-Seeking
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Substance use disorder (SUD) is associated with long-term changes in gene expression. While it is well established that upstream regulators, such as transcription factors and epigenetic modifications, mediate these changes in neurons, how cocaine use impacts other cell types in the brain is unknown. The aim of this research is to establish how transcriptional regulation in astrocytes changes the molecular landscape to support addiction related behaviors, specifically cocaine drug-seeking.

title: Training Program in Infectious Diseases in the Immunocompromised Host
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Infectious diseases are particularly dangerous for individuals with compromised immune systems due to cancer, transplantation or other conditions, or medications that weaken the immune system. Immunocompromised persons are more susceptible to initial infection, less capable of successfully battling infections, and therefore more likely to have severe complications from infections that are rarely seen in the general population. We seek continued support to train two new physician-scientists and/or post-doctoral PhD scientists each year for three years in three research areas that will advance knowledge of the biology, pathogenesis, diagnosis, prevention, and treatment of infectious diseases in immunocompromised persons.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Diastolic heart failure (dHF) and Alzheimer’s disease (AD) are both prevalent diseases where there is a poor understanding of the underlying mechanisms behind the disease. application_id: 11088937
activity_code: T32
title: Interdisciplinary Training in Microbial Pathogenesis and Immunology
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE/PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE
Identifying solutions for the treatment of microbial infections, aberrant immunological diseases, and other
emerging global health problems requires combined expertise and collaboration among scientists. A better
understanding of the mechanisms by which pathogens infect multicellular eukaryotic hosts and how hosts, in
turn, defend themselves against pathogens is critical for developing new alternative strategies to manage
microbial infections and better understand the function of the immune system. Real world solutions are
possible by investing in the training of new investigators to become facile in thinking and working across

application_id: 11033840
activity_code: K01
title: Mechanisms of Innate Immune Activation in Mitochondrial Disease Onset and Progression
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project narrative Genetic mitochondrial diseases (GMD) impact more than 1 in 4,000 people and are the most common cause of inherited metabolic disorders and among the most common causes of inherited neurological disease. GMDs are a significant medical challenge, as due to poor understanding of the mechanisms underlying these conditions there are no clinically proven therapies exist. The aim of this proposal is to follow up on recently published findings that innate immune cells play a key role in the pathogenesis of Leigh syndrome, the most common childhood GMD, by interrogating the innate immune mechanism signaling mechanisms that governing onset and progression of neurological disease in a mouse model of GMD.

application_id: 11034060
activity_code: F30
title: High physical activity in older adults with pain: behavioral and neural drivers of pain resilience
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Chronic musculoskeletal pain in older age is common and often leads to lower physical activity in this population; however, some older adults with pain are physically active. This proposal investigates the neural and behavioral measures of motivation that may protect against the detrimental effects of pain on physical activity. The results of this study may help identify modifiable protective factors and inform future targets to promote pain resiliency in older adults.

postdoctoral trainees have finished their training and hold academic or research positions at leading
universities and research institutions. In this continuation application, we will continue to focus our training
program on molecular epidemiology of cancer with new directions on human microbiota, epigenome, post
genome-wide gene-environment interactions, and integration of behavior and molecular epidemiology.

title: Survivorship Trajectories After Liver Transplantation: Identifying At‐Risk Survivors Needing Intervention
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Liver transplantation (LT) is the only curative treatment for patients with end-stage liver disease; however, it is also life-changing, and survivors endure complex physical, emotional, and psychological challenges after transplant. Predicting who will thrive or languish after LT is challenging and existing clinical scores fail to capture the lived experience of going through transplantation. Guided by a conceptual model of survivorship, this proposal will map out trajectories of survivorship, identify at-risk phenotypes associated with worse clinical outcomes, and pilot a navigation intervention, thereby yielding critical data to inform future studies of an intervention to improve trajectories and outcomes for LT survivors.

title: Unraveling the role of molecular capacitors that obscure cryptic genetic variation in Astyanax mexicanus during the evolution of eye loss

title: Multimedia caregiver education program to improve outcomes for children with cancer in low-resource settings
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Each year, 400,000 children are diagnosed with cancer, with 85% in low- and middle-income countries
(LMICs) where there is a 60% survival disparity compared to high income countries. The largest single
contributor to this disparity is treatment abandonment. The current proposal seeks to reduce treatment
abandonment through the adaptation and evaluation of two digital health education strategies targeting
caregiver knowledge about cancer diagnosis and treatment, reducing a known barrier to completing cancer
therapy and therefore improving outcomes for children with cancer in LMICs.

activity_code: T32
title: Integrative Cancer Scholars Training Grant
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA
phr: Program Narrative
The Integrative Cancer Scholars (ICS) training program is an innovative comprehensive plan to prepare the
next generation of cancer researchers by emphasizing critical thinking skills, adapting novel technologies and
requiring a co-mentoring plan with clinical and basic science faculty mentors. The development of innovative
and impactful therapies must integrate information from clinical experience and fundamental discoveries of
basic science to understand and act on the abnormal transformations of key molecules or pathways in cancer.
ICS trainees are expected to produce original, creative and significant cancer research and have advanced
communication skills for dissemination of their discoveries.

application_id: 11033144
activity_code: K08
title: Identifying mechanisms of immune dysregulation via severe pediatric SARS-CoV-2
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative Immune dysregulation underlies many forms of pediatric critical illness, including severe manifestations of SARS-CoV-2 infection. Efforts to effectively identify and treat children with infection-related immune dysregulation have been hindered by gaps in knowledge of the mechanisms connecting pathogen exposure to severe disease. This proposal leverages state-of-the-art immune profiling technologies and a unique cohort of children with life-threatening manifestations of SARS-CoV-2 to elucidate some of these mechanisms, towards improved outcomes for children particularly vulnerable to common infections.

application_id: 11129682
activity_code: K23
title: Longitudinal associations of maternal mobile device use and maternal-infant wellbeing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Adoption and use of mobile devices have each grown exponentially in the last decade and mounting evidence
suggests that mobile devices may uniquely interfere with a parent’s ability to sensitively respond to their child’s
cues. These sensitive interactions are especially important in infancy and for low-income families, creating the
foundations for social-emotional development that last a lifetime. This proposal seeks to address gaps in
knowledge by understanding how first-time, low-income mothers use mobile devices, and the potential
associations of mobile device use with maternal wellbeing, infant regulation, and maternal-infant co-regulation,
in order to ultimately promote the health of children in infancy and beyond.

title: Mechanisms of pathogenic gene activation by aberrant transcriptional hubs formed by mutant ENL
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Aberrant transcriptional hub formation, defined by high local concentrations of proteins, has been associated with disease, including cancers and human expansion repeat disease, yet the exact functional consequence of hub formation remains unknown. org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although race has no biological basis, racism is a known socioenvironmental stressor that has dire effects on physical and mental health in Black Americans. Here, we aim to quantify the neurobiological impact of perceived racism on behavioral and functional correlates of the medial temporal hippocampal system, a region highly sensitive to socioenvironmental stress and implicated in stress regulation and learning and memory. Understanding whether perceived racism impacts MTH neurocognitive integrity will allow researchers to develop individualized interventions, as well as in the future provide evidence to policy makers to direct funding to underserved, under-resourced communities to mitigate the impact of socioenvironmental factors such as racism.

application_id: 11166551
activity_code: K23
title: The role of the microbiome in hepatic encephalopathy pathogenesis and treatment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
One-third of patients with cirrhosis will develop a complication called hepatic encephalopathy, which is
characterized by confusion, fatigue, and personality changes – all of which significantly decrease quality of life
and patient survival. Current treatments are only moderately effective, but there is some data suggesting that
intestinal bacteria may play an important role in causing hepatic encephalopathy. This project uses novel
approaches to identify the importance of intestinal bacteria in hepatic encephalopathy, and tests if treatments
that influence intestinal bacteria can treat this condition.

title: A Novel Postoperative Prognostic Liquid Biopsy: Tumor-Associated cfDNA and Leukocyte Analysis in Oropharyngeal Cancer Surgical Drain Fluid
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Subjective clinical assessments of patient risk in HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (OPC) could be improved with our novel objective liquid biopsy assay of the inflammatory fluid that collects in Jackson Pratt surgical drains. application_id: 10767936
activity_code: K01
title: Mechanisms of Asymmetric Cell Division in Developmental Hematopoiesis
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative This proposal seeks to elucidate the cellular and molecular mechanisms that control the balance between self-renewal of blood progenitors and differentiation of mature blood cell types. Understanding how this balance is established during normal development and maintained during stress is relevant to blood disorders characterized by the production of disproportionate blood cell types such as fatal myeloproliferative disorder, anemia, and immune dysfunction upon aging. This work will help establish a model system that will allow for real-time monitoring of the blood system and ultimately the identification of key factors that regulate cell division and cell fate during hematopoiesis.

application_id: 11014657
activity_code: DP2
title: Understanding Metabolism in Space and Time – Mechanistic Analysis of the Dynamic Spatial Organization of Metabolism
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: All living organisms are dynamic metabolic entities emerging from the collective behavior of individual
biomolecules through space and time. As much of our effort has been to identify and characterize these
individuals, we are largely in dark about the spatial-temporal organization accommodating their activities. This
proposal will integrate advanced imaging technologies, deep learning-based analyses, and latest synthetic
protein tools to 1) mathematically define subcellular organization at nanometric isotropic resolution and 2)
systematically address how subcellular landscape can collectively steer individual activities to impact metabolic
outcomes.

title: Intestinal mitochondrial dysfunction and the gut-brain-immune axis in models of Parkinson's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Parkinson’s Disease may begin in the gut. This research will determine how damage to mitochondria in the intestine can contribute to the spread of Parkinson’s Disease pathology (α-Synuclein) from the gut to the brain. Findings from this research may lead to new ways to diagnose Parkinson’s Disease earlier, as well as new ways to treat both damage to neurons in the brain and gastrointestinal symptoms through therapies directed at the gut.

application_id: 10834009
activity_code: F32
title: Characterizing the connectivity and molecular composition of opioid-sensitive neurons in the periaqueductal gray
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative Opioids are powerful analgesics, but their harmful side effects have led to a nationwide opioid epidemic. Neurons in the periaqueductal gray (PAG) contribute to the pain-relieving and rewarding effects of opioids, but the organization of these circuits and how they contribute to opioid-induced behaviors remains unclear. To elucidate how opioids affect PAG neurons, I will characterize the organization of PAG neural circuits and investigate how they contribute to opioid-induced behaviors using input-output circuit mapping, single-cell transcriptomics, and circuit-specific neuromodulation experiments in freely moving animals.

application_id: 10800650
activity_code: F32
title: Deconstructing epileptic circuits in a mouse model of SLC6A1 syndrome
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The SLC6A1 gene, which encodes the GABA transporter GAT1, is implicated in a spectrum of neurodevelopmental disorders including epilepsy, autism, and intellectual disability, collectively named SLC6A1 syndrome. Although the cellular and circuit mechanisms by which SLC6A1 mutations cause SLC6A1 syndrome remain unknown, my preliminary data show state-dependent seizures in thalamocortical circuits in a novel mouse model of SLC6A1 syndrome. Here I will use electrophysiological and genetic tools to determine whether thalamic circuits are key regulators of epileptic seizures associated with SLC6A1 syndrome, with the aim to eventually aid the clinical management of network dysfunction in SLC6A1 syndrome.

application_id: 11159056
activity_code: DP2
title: Quantifying the dynamics of gene regulation and nuclear organization during embryogenesis
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 All the cells that compose our bodies arise from a single fertilized ovum and contain the same genomic
information, thus during embryonic development the correct genes must be turned on and off in the right cells at
the right times to grow and maintain an individual capable of independent life. The work proposed here will
integrate cutting edge techniques to directly visualize and quantify how this regulation of gene expression is
orchestrated during embryonic development. The critical new information that will be gained from the proposed
experiments have the potential to lead to novel therapeutic approaches to prevent or repair defects that arise
from aberrant gene expression during development, in aging, and in cancer.

title: The role of beta2-adrenergic receptor and interleukin-6 signaling in macrophage-driven choroidal neovascularization
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Evidence suggests a role for pro-angiogenic macrophages in neovascular age-related macular degeneration pathogenesis, but currently there are no therapies to specifically target these cells. Our preliminary data demonstrates that beta-adrenergic receptor antagonism inhibits laser- induced choroidal neovascularization by reducing interleukin-6 expression in macrophages. We aim to dissect the beta-adrenergic receptor -> interleukin-6 -> angiogenesis pathway, and to determine how beta-adrenergic receptor inhibition influences the transcriptional profile of macrophages in order to identify new therapeutic targets that will inhibit macrophage-driven angiogenesis.

application_id: 11170631
activity_code: K01
title: Evaluation of clinical and social factors associated with deficit accumulation trajectories in older survivors with early-stage non-small cell lung cancer
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project narrative
Aging involves a gradual clinical decline in cognitive and physical function and often the development of
various comorbidities. However, significant life events, such as a cancer diagnosis, cancer treatment, or other
individual-level and societal-level factors can accelerate clinical decline and aging in cancer survivors
compared to other older people without a cancer diagnosis. The completion of this study will identify targetable
multilevel aging risk factors in the older population with lung cancer, as well as provide a valuable monitoring
tool to predict dynamic risk of aging-related clinical decline, allowing for a deeper understanding of drivers of
aging acceleration and timely intervention before clinical decline occurs.

application_id: 11023078
activity_code: F30
title: How neural signals are integrated over time
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Loss of spatial memories may be one of the most distressing symptoms of neurological disorders such as
Alzheimer’s disease and psychiatric disorders such as schizophrenia. Patients with Alzheimer’s disease may
struggle to remember landmarks or form cognitive maps of their environment; patients with schizophrenia may
struggle with sensory integration, leading to perceptual disturbances and impairments in spatial working
memory. This project will characterize cellular- and circuit-level mechanisms of spatial integration, which will
lead to a better understanding of how these processes malfunction in disease.

title: Characterizing DC-specific mechanisms of LNP immunogenicity to enhance LNP-based anti-tumor therapies
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dendritic cells (DCs) have been implicated as the critical mediator of the demonstrated efficacy of lipid
nanoparticle (LNP)-based COVID-19 vaccines but the exact molecular and cellular mechanisms of LNP
immunogenicity in DCs remain unclear. The outlined proposal seeks a) to elucidate such mechanisms by
characterizing changes in transcriptional program, phenotype and function of dendritic cells (DCs) upon LNP
uptake and payload expression of LNPs and b) to devise bi-specific antibody-based strategies to improve DC-
targeting in vivo. In achieving the aims of the proposal, we hope to pave the way forward to more rational
designs of LNP formulations as well as enhanced targeting strategies to modulate DCs to enhance anti-tumor
immunity.

application_id: 11162701
activity_code: F32
title: B Cells in Preeclampsia: Long-term Effects on Offspring
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MISSISSIPPI MED CTR
phr: During pregnancy, women can develop a disease called preeclampsia (PE) that is characterized by new-onset
hypertension, chronic inflammation, activated B cells secreting autoantibodies that active the angiotensin II type
1 receptor (AT1-AA), fetal growth restriction, and increased postpartum risk for disease for both mother and
child. The frequency of PE has increased in the last 20 years, but there has been no change in standard of care
in the last 50 years; therefore, there is an urgent need to find new therapies for these women. This proposal
aims to test a novel therapeutic that targets AT1-AA to improve maternal outcomes and reduce the postpartum
risks of the disease for women who had PE and their children.

application_id: 11145758
activity_code: K99
title: Transcriptional regulation of oligodendroglial differentiation
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Oligodendrocyte precursor cells (OPCs) are the most abundantly mitotic cell in the brain and give rise to oligodendrocytes responsible for myelination through the process of OPC differentiation, which is critical to neurodevelopment and maintenance of myelination, yet the processes mediating this constancy are unclear. Oligodendroglia dynamics require the precise timing of transcription factors expression, that is essential for efficiently remyelinating lesions. The proposed research will determine genetic mechanisms through which the main circadian factor BMAL1 and other key transcription factors control OPC differentiation during myelination, providing critical insights into both normal and aberrant myelination associated with the demyelinating neurological disorder multiple sclerosis.

application_id: 11118804
activity_code: T32
title: Cancer Epigenetics Training (CET) Program
org_name: VAN ANDEL RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Cancer Epigenetics Training Program at Van Andel Research Institute (VARI) will prepare postdoctoral
fellows to become independent research leaders in the field of cancer epigenetics. The program will accomplish
this by offering cutting-edge, interdisciplinary research training in structural and functional aspects of epigenetics,
complemented by state-of-the-art institutional resources, unique professional development activities, and
integration with established translational research programs and partnerships led by VARI investigators.
Trainees will emerge from the program with comprehensive training in cancer epigenetics and a professional
foundation from which to launch their independent research careers.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it will advance mechanistic understanding of
neuromuscular adaptation to exercise in relevant AD mouse models and the roles of a key regulator of cellular
energetics in AD-like etiology, a pathology for which there are few treatment options. The project is relevant to
NIA’s mission because it focuses on the molecular and physiological response of skeletal muscle mitochondria
and motor nerves in the context of AD neuropathology that indicates a maladaptive bioenergetic response to
exercise, which may elucidate an integrated mechanism that will take into account the system-dependent nuance
of bioenergetic dysregulation in AD etiology. Training in measures of nerve activity in rodents and humans and

title: Brain-Behavior Mechanisms of Social Cognition in First-Episode Psychosis
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Social cognitive impairments are highly prominent in psychotic disorders and have been linked to social and
functional outcomes beyond the impact of other illness-related factors (i.e., psychiatric symptoms, nonsocial
cognition), yet available treatments for social and social cognitive dysfunction are limited in their effectiveness.
Despite recognition as an area of clinical relevance and unmet need, the brain-behavior mechanisms that give
rise to social cognitive impairment in psychosis remain poorly characterized, thus limiting our attempts to target
primary sources of observed dysfunction. The proposed study will examine the neural bases of mentalizing, a
key social cognitive process in psychosis, and its association with clinical and real-world functioning in a
transd...

application_id: 11033930
activity_code: K08
title: Hippocampal-cortical mechanisms of altered memory consolidation in epilepsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Memory is one of the most fundamental human cognitive functions, and the loss of memory is a devastating consequence in epilepsy, which affects more than 65 million people worldwide. In this application, I will combine single-neuron recordings from the hippocampus and cortex with advanced neural signal processing techniques in a rat model of epilepsy to investigate how memories are impaired in epilepsy and to test two potential therapies for ameliorating impairment. This will have important relevance for basic neuroscience, clinical neurophysiology, and targeted therapeutics for memory in epilepsy.

title: Addressing cancer challenges through impactful training
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: Addressing Rural cancer Inequities through Scientific Excellence (ARISE) is a postdoctoral training program at the University of Kentucky that will be a collaboration among the Center for Health, Engagement and Transformation (CHET), the NCI-designated Markey Cancer Center (MCC), Center for Clinical and Translational Science (CCTS), UK Center for Appalachian Research in Environmental Sciences (UK-CARES), six colleges and 10 departments to provide interdisciplinary training in behavioral cancer research. title: Developing Clinical Benchmarks and Quality Indicators forLate Preterm Infants During the Birth Hospitalization
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Contemporary literature for the care of late preterm infants during the birth hospitalization is limited, and no current clinical benchmarks or quality measures exist for this population of 280,000 infants born per year in the United States. Significant variation exists in their clinical management, including inter-institutional variability in admission rates that has not been explained by clinical illness severity; this suggests overutilization of costly NICU care, which may lead to unnecessary separation of the mother-infant dyad. The proposed research establishes a rigorous understanding of the clinical needs of these infants, tests the association of structural variables to late preterm infant outcomes, and establishes both clinical benchmarks and quality markers for ...

activity_code: K99
title: Retinal circuit integration and degeneration in Neurofibromatosis type-1
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Neurofibromatosis type-1 (NF1) is a genetic predisposition to cancer syndrome. Effecting 1 in 3000 people, NF1 frequently leads to vision loss and the current treatment, chemotherapy rarely improves visual outcomes. In this proposal I aim to learn about the visual changes that occur in NF1 in a more severe animal model of the disease and to determine the potential side effects of cell replacement therapy to treat vision loss.

application_id: 10812353
activity_code: T32
title: Huntsman Cancer Institute (HCI) Cancer Genetics, Epigenetics, Models, and Signaling (Cancer GEMS) Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The Huntsman Cancer Institute (HCI) Cancer Genetics, Epigenetics, Models, and Signaling (Cancer GEMS) Training Program will support six PhD candidates each year. Students will participate in a two-year training program consisting of a combination of mentored research, didactic courses, career development activities, seminars, and conferences, all aimed at preparing these young scientists for the next stage of their careers in cancer research. This program will produce a cadre of new scientists capable of working with physicians to develop improved and individualized methods of cancer prevention, early detection/interception, and treatment.

application_id: 11129722
activity_code: T32
title: Huntsman Cancer Institute (HCI) Cancer Genetics, Epigenetics, Models, and Signaling (Cancer GEMS) Training Program
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Huntsman Cancer Institute (HCI) Cancer Genetics, Epigenetics, Models, and Signaling (Cancer GEMS)
Training Program will support six PhD candidates each year. Students will participate in a two-year training
program consisting of a combination of mentored research, didactic courses, career development activities,
seminars, and conferences, all aimed at preparing these young scientists for the next stage of their careers in

phr: Narrative Neural circuits in the medial entorhinal cortex are critical for spatial navigation, learning, and memory. activity_code: T32
title: Multidisciplinary Training in Gastrointestinal Cancers
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Gastrointestinal cancers constitute the plurality of cancer deaths in the United States, yet there remains a
pressing need to train the next generation of scientists to conduct GI cancer research in an innovative and
productive manner. Leveraging strong support from Columbia University Irving Medical Center and the Herbert
Irving Comprehensive Cancer Center, we have developed a GI Cancer Training Program to identify and recruit
a diverse pool of trainees, offer them a rigorous and individualized curriculum, and provide them with research

application_id: 11053547
activity_code: K01
title: Mechanisms of diet modulating experience-dependent amphetamine preference
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: NARRATIVE
Psychostimulant use disorder accounts for a third of overdose deaths, however, currently there is no FDA-
approved pharmacotherapy to treat it. Recent evidence suggests a role for diet and gut signaling in modulating
the dopaminergic reward signaling and SUD behavior. I propose to use a recently developed amphetamine
preference assay in Drosophila to identify the environmental factors, like dietary components, as well as signaling
pathways that modulate this SUD-related behavior.

application_id: 10820518
activity_code: F31
title: Uncovering Mechanisms of Risk-Variant APOL1-modulated Inflammatory Signaling in Macrophages
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative The G1 and G2 variants in the gene encoding Apolipoprotein L1 (APOL1) increase risk for developing focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) in people with recent African ancestry, but the exact mechanisms and agents driving the pathology are still being discovered. While the pathologic mechanisms of these variants in glomerular cells are actively being studied, the role of macrophage-mediated inflammation in the development of APOL1 kidney disease is still poorly understood. The research outlined in this proposal will use congenic induced pluripotent stem cell(iPSC)-derived macrophages to elucidate the precise mechanisms that increase macrophage inflammation and subsequent kidney injury, and provide a model to study other genetic drivers of inflammation in macrophages.

application_id: 11112514
activity_code: T32
title: Training in Research on Addictions in Interdisciplinary NeuroAIDS (TRAIN)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Substance abuse and HIV infection are highly prevalent, commonly co-occur, and have adverse effects on brain
structure and function, but too few scientists are properly trained to study these important areas. The “Training
on Addictions in Interdisciplinary NeuroAIDS (TRAIN)” program has been highly successful in its first two cycles
in recruiting and preparing remarkably productive and culturally diverse pre- and post-doctoral scholars to
conduct cutting edge research in this field. In seeking a second renewal of TRAIN we propose building on this
success in preparing future leaders, with enhanced emphases on the biologic mechanisms by which HIV and
substance use affect the CNS, as well as other factors that may improve or worsen these problems.

Addressing this burden requires state-of-the-art epidemiologic research to detect and monitor trends, and to identify risk factors in order to inform interventions to reduce the toll of substance abuse on individuals and society. Since its start in 2012, the Substance Abuse Epidemiology Training Program (SAETP) at Columbia University Medical Center has been training productive, successful junior substance abuse epidemiologists; in a renewal cycle, we propose to continue such training, making the public health relevance of this program very
strong.

application_id: 11062821
activity_code: DP2
title: Probing functioning lung at the cellular resolution in health and disease
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: Project Narrative:
The lack of effective therapeutics for many key pulmonary diseases, exemplified by the ongoing COVID-19
pandemic, demonstrates the urgent need to better understand the cellular dynamics of disease pathogenesis,
and to identify novel therapeutic targets in a realistic microenvironment. We will develop a model system in which
the mouse and human lung will be visualized and mechanistically probed in real-time and at the cellular level
while the respiratory and vascular functions of the lung are preserved. This technology will be utilized to (i)
deepen our understanding of multi-step progression of the pulmonary diseases such as cancer metastasis and
viral infection, and (ii) provide a realistic environment to identify therapeutic targets and test the response to
therapy.

application_id: 10830968
activity_code: T32
title: Training in genetic and neurobehavioral mechanisms of addiction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
Substance use disorders (SUD) are heritable and facilitated by a host of brain-based mechanisms. These
mechanisms, their neural correlates and genetic underpinnings, are increasingly being investigated in the
context of large consortium efforts using novel technologies including genomics and neuroimaging. Leveraging
such data at scale to address questions in addiction science is a challenge that this mixed
predoctoral/postdoctoral training program will rise to meet through a central focus on neurobehavioral
measures, genetically informative designs, and cutting edge quantitative methods to uncover and characterize
neurobehavioral mechanisms in the development of addiction.
