title: Effects of Genetic and Pharmacological Kynurenine Pathway Suppression on Healthspan, Lifespan, and Cellular Changes Associated with Aging in Mice
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: The kynurenine pathway (KP) is a conserved molecular pathway which consists of several bioactive metabolites that are detrimentally elevated with inflammation, aging and frailty in mammals. The goal of this study is to determine how suppression of KP metabolites affects functional decline, characteristics of aging, and lifespan in mice. In order to meet these goals, we will genetically and pharmacologically suppress specific components of the KP and measure functional decline, pathophysiological metabolic changes, lifespan and cellular changes associated with aging in mice.

title: Deciphering Determinants of Somatic Evolution with Single-Cell Genealogy
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Cells within the human body undergo continuous evolution, accruing heritable alterations as they proliferate and differentiate into diverse cell types over time, forming a complex cellular genealogy. application_id: 11054221
activity_code: K99
title: Structural and Functional Alteration of host RBCs by Babesia
org_name: NEW YORK BLOOD CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Babesia is an emerging zoonotic tick-borne parasite, with a fast-expanding geographical range of human infections globally, and a heavy prevalence in northeastern USA. Little is known about how the parasite remodels its host red blood cell (RBC) during infection, although clinical complications of the disease are linked to these changes. The current study aims to understand the nature and functionality of parasite proteins exported to the RBCs and detail their interactions with the RBC cytoskeleton and examine how this impacts RBC morphology and stability, all of which ultimately affect the pathological outcomes of the disease.

application_id: 11234192
activity_code: F99
title: From T Cells to Dendritic Cells: Mechanistic Dissection of CTLA4 and TIM3 in Cancer Immunity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
ICR blockade immunotherapies targeting PD1, CTLA4, and Lag3 have revolutionized cancer treatment, yet
they benefit only a small subset of patients and often lead to autoimmune toxicities, highlighting the need for
better mechanistic understanding of checkpoint signaling pathways. While adaptive immunity, especially T cell
responses, has been the primary focus, there is growing recognition of the innate immune system’s role in anti-
tumor activity. My F99/K00 projects seek to elucidate how the two pivotal checkpoint receptors, CTLA4 and
TIM3, function in adaptive and innate immunity respectively, to advance our mechanistic insights and
potentially uncover new strategies for cancer immunotherapy.

application_id: 11238582
activity_code: DP2
title: SPATIALLY MAPPING OF POOLED IN VIVO CRISPR SCREENS IN THE TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: RFA-RM-21-016: New Innovator Award– Project Narrative. Jellert T. Gaublomme
PROJECT NARRATIVE
My research proposal aims to develop and apply an approach that vastly expands our fundamental knowledge
of the gene circuitry that orchestrates pathogenic cellular behavior in the native tissue context. In particular, I will
perform in vivo pooled CRISPR perturbations at scale in tumor cells, and map spatially how these perturbations
alter the interactions with the immune system. Our approach will identify perturbations that stimulate immune
system mediated clearing of the tumor, which in turn will inform and prioritize therapeutic targets and modalities,
and is poised to generate knowledge to enhance health, lengthen the lifespan of patients, and reduce their
illness.

activity_code: F31
title: The molecular characterization of sex-specific piRNA transcription and snRNA transcription in C. elegans
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative PIWI-interacting RNA (piRNA)-mediated silencing of transposable elements is critical in preserving the integrity of the genome in germ cells, with the loss of the piRNA pathway leading to sterility and potentially aging and cancer. Recent studies indicate that piRNAs are expressed in a sexually dimorphic manner and yet the mechanisms by which this occurs remains poorly understood. In this proposal, I aim to characterize the sex- specific transcription complexes that uniquely drive the expression of male and female piRNAs and explore the surprising molecular link to the transcription of snRNAs of the spliceosome.

application_id: 11162435
activity_code: K99
title: The Role of the PBN Melanocortin System in the Regulation of Ingestive Behaviors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The parabrachial nucleus serves as a crucial sensory hub, relaying data from internal and external senses to
shape motivated behaviors; in this intricate network, the activation of melanocortin 3 and 4 receptors (MC3R and
MC4R) expressing neurons can either stimulate or suppress ingestive behaviors. Of particular importance, the
PBN MC3R neurons represent a novel population in the PBN as they contradict the overall role of the PBN in
inhibiting feeding. Understanding how these two neuronal populations in the PBN integrate opposing ingestive
behaviors is the major goal of this project.

application_id: 11033312
activity_code: K99
title: Structured quantification of inherited macular disease phenotypes as the basis for automated algorithms to determine causal genes
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The ability to obtain a molecular diagnosis in patients is essential for understanding the cellular etiology, clinical prognosis, and treatment prospects of inherited macular dystrophy —a heterogeneous group of genetic disorders characterized by progressive central vision loss. Genetic testing has recently become more accessible to patients with IMD, however, testing outcomes are often inconclusive due to the difficult task of identifying a single causal gene across the immense and complex landscape of the human genome. Using advanced diagnostic retinal imaging modalities, this research proposal will decipher precise phenotypic differences between IMD genes and create a prediction tool to help improve genetic testing outcomes for patients.

application_id: 10918109
activity_code: K08
title: Improving Interhospital Transfer of Patients with Neurologic Emergencies
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Interhospital transfer, the process of a patient being moved from one hospital to another for advanced and/or specialized care or patient preference, is occurring more frequently but the current process has many problems. These include poor communication between the doctors, poor communication between doctors and the patient and family, and inconsistent clinical practices. We will use methods from engineering and design to understand the reasons for these problems, get help from doctors, nurses, paramedics, patients, and families to create solutions, and test those solutions in two hospitals.

application_id: 11125858
activity_code: T32
title: Training in Auditory and Vestibular Neuroscience
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
Hearing and balance disorders affect a large percentage of our population. About 30-35 percent
of adults between the ages of 65 and 75 years have a hearing loss, and it is estimated that
about 50 million US citizens experience tinnitus in their lifetime. Balance disorders are also
prevalent in the population, particularly amongst the elderly. More than one in three people age
65 years or older fall each year, and falls are the leading cause of hospitalization amongst the
elderly. The proposed program will train the next generation of scientists to provide cures for
hearing and balance disorders.

application_id: 10918121
activity_code: DP5
title: CAR-based approaches for the treatment of Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Alzheimer's disease is a neurodegenerative disorder that affects an approximate 6.2 million Americans and results in more than 120,000 death per year in the United States alone. In four years, the incidence is expected to increase by 22% and by 2050 its will nearly double. The pathology is hypothesized to initiated by abnormal deposition of beta-amyloid (A13) and progresses with the abnormal phosphorylation and deposition of tau. Despite its economic and societal impact, no drug has been able to prevent or halt progression of the disease. In this study, we will identify novel treatment approaches that have the potential to provide lasting Al3 clearance and prevention of Al3 deposition.

application_id: 11065412
activity_code: F30
title: Structural basis for deficient actin mechanosensing in ACTN4-driven podocytopathy
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative
Almost half of the identified genes underlying hereditary podocytopathies, which are clinically characterized by
steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome, encode for proteins involved in actin cytoskeletal functions. Mutations in
α-actinin-4, a mechanically regulated actin crosslinker expressed in podocytes, cause focal segmental
glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). This proposal aims to elucidate structural mechanisms of deficient actin
mechanosensing by FSGS-causing α-actinin-4 variants via cryo-EM, and completing this project will advance
mechanistic insights into how impairments in actin cytoskeletal functions drive podocytopathies.

application_id: 11057504
activity_code: F32
title: Dissecting regulation and function of unc-6/Netrin via the generation of cis-regulatory alleles
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: Project Narrative UNC-6/Netrin plays vital roles in the directing of neuronal axons to their target cells, as well as in the formation of synapses, the connections between neurons that permit communication between them. It is unknown, though, whether the neurons that express unc-6/Netrin for the purpose of axon pathfinding are the same as those that express unc-6/Netrin to facilitate synapse formation. I propose to use the roundworm C. elegans to determine whether these two functions are separable and to characterize neuron-specific control of unc-6/Netrin expression, which may help me to elucidate the relative contribution of the lack of each of these functions in unc-6/Netrin-associated neurodevelopmental diseases.

org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative: Adoptive cellular therapies have limited efficacy in solid tumors which account for 90% of adult cancers. title: Developing long-term neuro-behavioral recording and real-time processing platforms for naturally behaving animals
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Much has been learned about neural circuitry and function; however, we still lack a

integrates complex behavioral measures with months-long, uninterrupted, wireless neural

openly and in real-time through a novel collaborative data sharing framework.

application_id: 11031321
activity_code: F31
title: The role of neonatal ROS in cardiomyocyte maturation and injury response
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS) have a well-documented association with a variety of disease pathologies, ROS also serve as key signaling molecules. Moreover, cardiac preconditioning has been effective in attenuating damage induced by ischemic injury, suggesting a beneficial role of ROS in priming cardiomyocytes for injury response. The goal of this proposal is to elucidate how diminished ROS during the initial neonatal mouse period impairs cardiomyocyte regeneration, as well as how lowered ROS levels during this period can delay metabolic maturation.

application_id: 10816425
activity_code: F30
title: Dissecting functional subgroups and closed-loop circuits between the pedunculopontine nucleus and the basal ganglia
org_name: GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Neural degeneration of the cholinergic neurons within the pedunculopontine nucleus (PPN) correlates with the severity of motor impairment in Parkinson's disease (PD). However, the role of the PPN within the basal ganglia circuit for motor output has not yet been comprehensively dissected making it unclear how its degeneration contributes to motor deficits in PD. This study is relevant to the mission of NIH because it aims to characterize the synaptic properties, regional connectivity, and closed-loop circuits between the major inhibitory basal ganglia nuclei, SNr and GPe, and rostral and caudal PPN to suggest new therapeutic targets.

application_id: 11143621
activity_code: F31
title: Functional interrogation of region-specific reactive astrocyte factors in neuroinflammation
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Inflammation is ubiquitous in all damage and disease in the central nervous system. Astrocytes in the brain
and spinal cord become reactive in response to inflammation, losing key homeostatic functions and gaining
harmful functions which may contribute to the progression of neurological disease and degeneration. While
previously thought to be a homogenous response, our preliminary data indicates striking brain region
differences in inflammatory astrocyte reactivity, and the proposed research will investigate the significance of
two region-specific reactive astrocyte factors in neuro-immune interactions, thermoregulation, and behavior.

application_id: 10816570
activity_code: K99
title: The role of the novel interface cell state in melanoma invasion
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Tumor and microenvironment cells often interact to promote cancer progression, but gaps remain in our
understanding of how these interactions occur. My recent publication uncovered a novel cilia-enriched
“interface” cell state adopted by both tumor and microenvironment cells in melanoma, indicating that the
interface may have a critical role in melanoma progression. My proposed work will investigate how the
interface is established and how it contributes to invasion, and could provide novel therapeutic targets to
prevent melanoma progression.

title: Research Training in Child Behavior and Nutrition
org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: Nutritional and dietary modifications are key components to the management of many pediatric chronic conditions yet are often difficult for youth and families to accomplish, causing significant morbidity and reduced life-expectancy. Clinical investigators with knowledge of behavioral, biomedical, nutritional, and clinical trials sciences are critically needed to develop empirically-tested prevention models and clinical interventions that improve dietary adherence, nutritional status, and long-term health and quality of life outcomes for youth. Graduates of this fellowship program will be national leaders who are team scientists and will have a significant impact on research in child behavior and nutrition and relevant health outcomes for many years to come.
terms: Child Behavior;Child Nutrition;Research Training

title: Program ARISE2: African mental health Researchers Inspired and Equipped
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CAPE TOWN
phr: ARISE NARRATIVE
Mental disorders make an increasingly significant contribution to the burden of disease in
African low- and middle-income countries, also increasing risk for a range of other health
conditions and injuries, yet health systems are poorly resourced to address the problem.
Although investment in mental health research capacity building in Africa has increased in
recent years, disparities in these areas remain evident particularly in comparison to the
substantial funding underpinning research and research training for infectious and other non-
communicable diseases in the region. Thus, there is a need for mental health research
capacity building in Africa, led by African public mental health researchers, to drive local
research priorities and retain precious human resources on ...

application_id: 11075299
activity_code: T32
title: Training in Obesity Research
org_name: LSU PENNINGTON BIOMEDICAL RESEARCH CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Obesity is a complex disease that occurs through genetic and epigenetic mechanisms and their interactions
with the environment. The objective of the Training in Obesity Research Program at Pennington Biomedical
Research Center is to train the highest caliber of postdoctoral fellows to understand the complex interactions
between genetic, molecular, physiological, behavioral and environmental aspects of obesity and become
productive research scientists in obesity with the capability of establishing successful scientific careers in
academia, academic medicine, governmental agencies, and in the private sector.
terms: <Obesity><Research><Training><adiposity><corpulence>

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: UW STAR PROGRAM – PROJECT NARRATIVE
The expressed purpose of the UW STAR Program is to provide students from underrepresented
populations (i.e., African American, Latino/Hispanic, Native American/Alaska Native, and
Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander); first generation college students, economically and
educationally disadvantaged students and students with disabilities with short-term, intensive
summer research experiences that will strongly encourage and support matriculation into
graduate and professional educational pathways (i.e., MD, MD/PhD, PhD, and MS degree
tracks) in NHLBI-focused cardiovascular, pulmonary, and hematologic research.

application_id: 11138909
activity_code: D43
title: Mali-Northwestern University Breast Cancer Prevention Research Training Program (MN-BCP)
org_name: UNIV OF SCIENCES, TECH & TECH OF BAMAKO
phr: NARRATIVE
In Africa, only a few scientists possess the necessary training in epidemiology, molecular pathology,
biostatistics, and implementation science to conduct population-based and mechanistic studies on breast
cancer prevention. This research training program aims to address this gap by leveraging over a decade of
collaboration between Malian and U.S. investigators at Northwestern University and the University of Chicago.
The goal of this Mali-Northwestern University Breast Cancer Prevention (MN-BCP) research training program
is to develop and implement contextually appropriate and culturally adaptive strategies for breast cancer
prevention with the aims to advance breast cancer research and improve public health outcomes in Mali and
other similar settings.

application_id: 11197329
activity_code: DP1
title: Molecular and Cellular Subtyping of Response to Drugs of Abuse in Co-morbid Psychiatric Disorders
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 This research investigates how cellular responses to drugs are affected by genetic loading for psychiatric
disorders. Using high-throughput screening, patient-derived cell models and clinical data, and machine learning,
this project systematically examines multiple risk factors in combination rather than in isolation, better reflecting
the complexity of real-world addiction. This work has the potential to unravel molecular pathways at the interface
of addiction and psychiatric disorders, setting up a new paradigm for developing addiction treatments in the
greater context of patients’ mental health.

application_id: 11136216
activity_code: F30
title: Paternal age effect mutations as selfish drivers of germline stem cell clonality
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mean paternal age in the United States has been increasing for the past 40 years. At the same time, there is
increasing evidence that advanced paternal age correlates with negative effects for offspring. The goal of this
project is to use clonal analysis in the zebrafish testis to expand our understanding of the biological mechanisms
of male fertility across the lifespan, with particular attention paid to the Paternal Age Effect (PAE) disorders that
cause severe developmental abnormalities in children.

application_id: 10753512
activity_code: K23
title: Clinical and Non-Invasive Biomarkers of Myotonic Dystrophy
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Myotonic dystrophy (DM) is the most frequent muscular dystrophy in adults with a prevalence of 1 in 8,000 people, it affects several organs such as skeletal muscle, heart, and brain, it is inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion, and no cure exists to date. Promising therapies are in current development to target the expanded RNA-mediated toxicity associated with this disease; however, reliable biomarkers to monitor disease activity and response to upcoming treatments are lacking. For this purpose, this research proposal aims at identifying non-invasive, painless, and quantitative biomarkers of DM muscle disease by using, in parallel but independently, muscle ultrasound and MRI, and electrical impedance myography at two-year interval in these patients.

title: miR-301b inhibits autophagy by directly targeting RAB5A
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM) is a disease that affects both adults and children with different etiologies. However, the therapeutic strategies in children with heart failure are based on adult clinical studies and are less effective in managing HF in children. The purpose of this study is to investigate the mechanistic role of miR-301b in regulating autophagy in cardiomyocytes and evaluate changes in autophagic markers in pediatric DCM hearts. This study underscores the importance of pediatric-focused research studies and is critical to identifying novel age-specific therapy in this group and improving the translational barrier roadblock that negatively affected pediatric heart failure patients.

application_id: 11190448
activity_code: DP1
title: Tuning the anterior cingulate from the outside in: a new way forward for treating mental health disorders and beyond
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
Dysfunction within the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex has been implicated in a large breadth of
brain disorders with psychiatric underpinnings. Targeting neuromodulation using focused
ultrasound in lieu of ablative strategies to this region holds promise as an important therapeutic
strategy, but substantial optimization is needed to make this a viable treatment. In addition, the
serendipitous adjacency of the dorsal anterior cingulate to the centrally located inferior sagittal
sinus provides an incredible opportunity to leverage this anatomical relationship to develop an
incisionless, intravascular ultrasound device-based strategy from bench to bedside.

title: The Role of Patient Capacity in Chronic Kidney Disease Trajectories
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Today, approximately 15% of the adult population in the United States live with chronic kidney disease (CKD),
some of which will need dialysis or transplant. Home dialysis and pre-emptive transplant, compared to in-
center dialysis, may be better for patients for numerous reasons. This research seeks to understand if patients’
capacity predicts their choice of dialysis, ability to stay on that type of dialysis, or their access to transplant.

application_id: 10910656
activity_code: DP2
title: Epigenetic drivers of quiescence in artemisinin-resistant Plasmodium falciparum malaria
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The emergence of artemisinin-resistant Plasmodium falciparum (Pf) parasites imperils global malaria treatment
and control. Quiescence plays a central role in artemisinin-resistant parasite survival, as well as in the parasite
starvation response, but drivers of entry into and exit from this cellular state remain unknown. The proposed
research will employ transcriptomics, mass spectrometry and genetic knockdowns to identify and characterize
novel epigenetic mechanisms that regulate drug-resistant parasite entry into and exit from quiescence, thereby
fundamentally altering how we understand parasite gene regulation and allowing for the development of novel
antimalarials to combat one of the deadliest global diseases.

Pneumonia remains the leading infectious cause of death. Presently doctors do not know which patients can
benefit from steroids, when to give steroids, or how much steroid to give. In this application, we propose an
approach to steroid use in pneumonia that considers an individual’s unique features, seeking to give the right

application_id: 10768669
activity_code: K23
title: Differential Susceptibility for Developing Chronic Post-Surgical Pain Across Sleep Trajectories and Inflammatory Presentations
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Insufficient and poor sleep is pervasive among individuals with shoulder conditions requiring surgical intervention. Shortened sleep and increased sleep disturbances induce an outsized inflammatory reaction, which sensitizes the nociceptive response and may increase susceptibility for developing chronic post-surgical pain and the subsequent need for prolonged postoperative opioid therapy. Therefore, this prospective study will utilize daily sleep data, collected from wearable devices, and inflammatory markers to examine associations with patient-reported pain outcomes and opioid utilization up to 3 months after shoulder surgery.

title: Integrated Training in Engineering and Diabetes
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Diabetes is a complex disease, and understanding its complexity is critical for creating new clinical therapies to control the current diabetes epidemic, better treat its complications, and lead to an eventual cure. Because engineering approaches have proven useful for solving complex biomedical problems, we propose to train a new multi-disciplinary work force with the skills needed to integrate both engineering and diabetes research approaches. title: Chemical glycobiology tools to decipher host-microbiota interactions
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Although the human microbiota has been broadly associated with improved outcomes for multiple diseases, the
development of microbiota-targeted medicines has remained largely stalled due to a lack of mechanistic
information that underlie these correlations. In this project, we will develop a suite of chemical tools to discover
and characterize physical interactions between microbiota, their glycan metabolites, and mammalian tissues.

biotherapeutics to ameliorate inﬂammatory diseases.

title: Uncovering the regulatory logic of gene expression encoded by disordered regions
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Transcription factors are proteins that determine which genes are turned on or off in a
cell at any given moment. Mutations in transcription factors cause many diseases

application_id: 11244299
activity_code: F32
title: The role of anterior cingulate leptin signaling in modulating effortful reward seeking
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Effort valuation, or the weighing of an effort’s cost against the value of a reward, is disrupted in a variety of
psychiatric disorders. The amount of energy available to perform effortful tasks is signaled in part by leptin, a fat-
secreted hormone that is disrupted in metabolic syndrome. This study explores whether leptin plays a role in
brain circuits that govern effort valuation, thereby improving our understanding of the relationship between
metabolic and mental health.

application_id: 11134539
activity_code: K99
title: The Role of Distinct Dorsal Column Nuclei Outputs in Persistent Pain
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: Project Narrative
Touch-evoked pain (allodynia) is a common symptom in persistent pain conditions, affecting a significant
percentage of individuals after surgeries (e.g. mastectomy, thoracotomy, hernia repair, cesarean) and
chemotherapy treatment. This project aims to determine how altered coding within the dorsal column nuclei, an
integral brainstem region for tactile processing, shapes the activity of downstream circuits to promote touch-
evoked pain following injury. An improved understanding of these circuits will facilitate the development of
improved therapeutics for the treatment of persistent pain.

application_id: 11214463
activity_code: K01
title: Tracking brain structural trajectories for early detection and prognosis of Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
Brain structural changes caused by Alzheimer's Disease (AD) are typically believed to be late in life, since the hallmark
pathological changes including beta-amyloid plaques and tau tangles are thought to develop over many years. However,
recent research suggests that these changes might start earlier than we once thought, which motivates us to explore the
possibility of spotting the AD's early signs in the structural change of the brain. Our research aims to create a
comprehensive map detailing how the brain naturally changes throughout life, which facilitates distinguishing normal
aging from the early stages of AD and identifying the transitions when patterns in brain aging signal a higher AD risk.

application_id: 10908838
activity_code: DP2
title: The Circadian Clock Coordinates Animal and Microbial Metabolism
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The biochemical transformation of our diets by the trillions of microorganisms that colonize our gut is essential
for our health and fitness. Recent work indicates that our circadian clocks coordinate functions of our
microbiomes with rhythmic feeding behaviors, however, the mechanisms that underlie coordination of our
metabolism with the metabolic functions of our microbiomes are not well understood. Here we propose to
mechanistically determine how circadian clocks synchronize animal and microbial metabolic activities to promote
overall health and fitness.

title: Decoding endocrine and paracrine communication through mammokines
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative Recent studies have confirmed that endocrine interactions play a significant role in causing and contributing to various common health issues, especially obesity, type 2 diabetes, and the resulting cardiovascular problems. application_id: 11171842
activity_code: DP2
title: Neural circuit mechanisms that support the transformation of sensory inputs into number coding
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative
People with dyscalculia, impaired numerical abilities, are facing significant challenges in their daily
lives because numerical abilities are essential for human social activities and career development.
Effective treatments for number-related disorders are missing due to the lack of our understanding of
the underlying neural mechanism for number sense. In this project, we will reveal the neural circuit
mechanisms underlying the generation and maintenance of number sense, which can guide the
development of more effective treatment for number-related disorders.

application_id: 10908855
activity_code: DP2
title: Understanding the principles of phosphene fusion via high-channel-count visual prostheses
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Blindness affects 40 million people globally, severely impairing quality of life and leading to economic losses of
$12.5 billion/annum in the US and $29 billion in developed economies worldwide. By delivering
microstimulation currents to the brain via a visual prosthesis, researchers aim to restore functional vision in
profoundly blind people. Our goal is to optimize the resolution and thereby the efficacy and interpretability of
artificial vision generated by a neuroprosthesis.

application_id: 11123286
activity_code: T32
title: University of Utah Interdisciplinary Training Program in Computational Approaches to Diabetes and Metabolism Research
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
To accelerate the pace of diabetes research, there is a pressing need to train computational scientists the
biological basis of diabetes and related metabolic disorders. The University of Utah Computational Approaches
to Diabetes and Metabolism Research Training Program trains both predoctoral and postdoctoral trainees in
the computational and mathematical sciences and in the biological basis of diabetes and obesity. These
bioinformatics scientist trainees will gain the expertise and leadership skills to apply computational methods to
complex biological questions that will ultimately impact the prevention, treatment, and outcomes of people with
diabetes and related metabolic diseases.

application_id: 10684528
activity_code: DP2
title: Capturing, quantifying, and understanding combinatorial effects in small molecule signaling
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
Chemical landscapes in biology are composed of thousands to millions of different small molecules, which
means that a cell or organism is always exposed to many different small molecules at the same time. The
overall goal of this project is to understand how biological outcomes may be driven by combinatorial effects of
multiple small molecules, with an initial focus on such effects in the context of host-microbiome communication.
This work may allow us to predict and manipulate effects of the microbiome on the human host and more
broadly will establish general models to describe signaling in complex chemical landscapes, which are
ubiquitous in biology.

application_id: 10909608
activity_code: DP2
title: Tracing the evolutionary history of human adaptive traits through ancient DNA
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
Modern human expanded worldwide and settled in different environments that subjected them to
selective pressure, driving genetic adaptations to varied local conditions. We are using ancient
DNA to contextualize the emergence of genomic adaptive variants that have undergone selection
in both now-extinct archaic and modern human populations in East Asia and understand how
these populations adapted to diverse environmental pressure, including climate, diets, and
infectious diseases. This project aims to enhance our understanding of the evolution of recent
human phenotypes by defining the genetic basis of human adaptive traits.

application_id: 11135349
activity_code: T32
title: Postdoctoral Training in Biomedical MRI and MRS
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
This application is for support of a comprehensive training program for postdoctoral trainees to
become experts in different aspects of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and spectroscopy
(MRS) to be able to contribute to their translational applications. MRI and MRS are very
important methods for the non-invasive diagnosis of diseases and monitoring of treatment in
clinical practice, and provide vital information for a wide range of other biomedical research
applications. This program will address the shortage of suitably qualiﬁed postdoctoral scientists
able to contribute to the further development of MR methods or to apply them effectively.

title: Training Clinical Scientists in Radiological Imaging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The UC San Diego Department of Radiology is submitting a competitive renewal of its postdoctoral training
program that focuses on developing clinician-scientists through a year of laboratory research followed by 32
weeks of protected research time during the subsequent 4 years of residency training. This program trains
radiologists to become clinical-translational research faculty, who ultimately can be competitive for NIH grant
funding, industry collaboration and entrepreneurship. Trainees will be supported by 12 radiology and 12 non‐
radiology mentors that together have 103 active grants and over $28M in grant support (current year funding).

phr: NARRATIVE
Training young scientists and engineers in translational research in biomaterials facilitates the translation of
biomaterials-based technologies to the clinic. Specifically, the Translational Research in Biomaterials
(TRB) curriculum enables students to develop the creative, analytical, quantitative, and professional skills
they need to make new discoveries that will revolutionize fields ranging from diagnostics and drug delivery
to tissue engineering. These advances will improve patient care in multiple areas of medicine and ensure an
academic and industrial workforce to meet the challenges of the 21st century.

title: Regulation of Protein Kinase C Theta by Phosphorylation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal seeks to elucidate the regulation of protein kinase C (PKC) theta () by phosphorylation and how
this impacts function and biology. I have selected several critical phosphorylation residues (T538, S662, S676,
S685, and S695) for further analysis with the goal of determining how each of these regulate PKC function and
signaling. These fundamental studies will offer novel insights into the regulation of PKC in T cells and platelets,
with application in immune and blood disorders.

application_id: 10974035
activity_code: K01
title: Long-term effects of today's medical care access policies on the future burden of Alzheimer's disease and related dementias
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The management of cardiovascular and metabolic health, including type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM), is an important part of healthy aging and affects risk of Alzheimer's disease and related dementias (ADRD). The proposed research aims to examine how patient cost burden, as measured in uninsured individuals and high deductible health plan (HDHP) enrollees, affects the management and progression of T2DM, potentially leading to long-term consequences for the incidence and burden of ADRD. This will be accomplished with an innovative simulation model, that connects existing models of T2DM progression and aging, to build understanding of the complicated interplay between health policies, healthy aging, and ADRD.

application_id: 10783043
activity_code: F31
title: Role of Sympathetic Innervation in Islet Plasticity during Pregnancy.
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative Dysfunction or loss of endocrine beta-cells leads to diabetes. Pancreatic islets receive neuronal input from sympathetic and parasympathetic nerves and these interactions are essential for hormone secretion. During pregnancy, beta-cells respond to high metabolic demand by increasing their mass and secretory function. The goal of this project is to address the role of sympathetic nerves in regulating the structural and functional plasticity of beta-cells during pregnancy using whole organ clearing, 3D imaging, and nerve ablation studies in mice.

application_id: 11172015
activity_code: DP2
title: Organic Pseudometals to Sustain and Transform Small-Molecule Drug Synthesis
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Over the past decades, transition metals have become indispensable for the discovery, optimization, and
manufacturing of pharmaceuticals, but these elements are toxic, expensive, and limited in supply. To meet the
urgent need for benign alternatives, we propose an unusual approach: the design of purely organic compounds
(redox-active molecules) that can function as “pseudometals.” These catalysts will be used not only to mimic the
essential reactivity metals for the synthesis of critical medicines, but they will also be rationally modified to access
new transformations currently inaccessible to conventional transition metals.

tumors using a novel biomaterial in conjunction with a split and programmable chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)

solid tumors, specifically poor T cell persistence in the tumor microenvironment, lack of specific antigens, and
treatment-limiting adverse effects. The capability of this novel therapeutic system to promote CAR T cell
proliferation, prevent antigen escape, and mitigate adverse events will be evaluated.

title: The cis-regulatory basis of lineage-specific phenotypes among human groups
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Genomic and phenotypic data from our closest human relatives, Neanderthals and Denisovans (archaic humans), is becoming ever more accessible due to technological advancements, but the relationship of specific genetic changes between human lineages and their downstream phenotypic effects is still unmapped. application_id: 11172142
activity_code: DP2
title: Engineering enhanced erythropoiesis for red blood cell disorders
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
While ex vivo genome editing of hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) may provide a lifelong cure for
red blood cell disorders like sickle cell disease, the requirement for a high percentage of corrected
HSCs in the bone marrow remains a significant barrier to achieving safe and effective translation
of genome editing. Here, we will develop multiple genome editing strategies to simultaneously
correct sickle cell disease and enhance erythropoiesis of functional erythroid cells, which may
lower the therapeutic threshold for disease correction. We then will deploy the most effective
strategies to mediate reduced-intensity myeloablation in the context of ex vivo genome editing
and to mediate enhanced in vivo genome editing for direct correction of HSCs in the bone marrow.

application_id: 10919219
activity_code: K01
title: Discovery of genetic modifiers of PTEN-ASD severity in a library of genetically diverse iPSC lines
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Common genetic variation is a substantial contributor to autism severity, yet the complicated genetic underpinnings of autism have hampered the discovery of disease modifying genes. Here, I will model a highly penetrant ASD mutation (PTEN) in a library of 200 genetically diverse iPSC lines from ASD and neurotypical individuals to identify regions of the genome which influence autism relevant cellular phenotypes. I will then explore the mechanisms by which genetic variants affect phenotypic outcomes in idiopathic ASD patients, from whom iPSC lines were generated, and in ~600 patients with various PTEN mutations.

application_id: 11221426
activity_code: K00
title: Inter-organellar communication in metabolic reprogramming of colorectal cancer
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Metabolic reprogramming is required in colorectal cancer to maintain cellular plasticity and to thrive in the anoxic lumen. There is a strong body of research that coordinated inter-organellar interactions of endoplasmic reticulum-mitochondria are essential for compartmentalized metabolism. The proposed research will fill a major gap in the fundamental understanding of the mechanisms in which endoplasmic reticulum-mitochondria drive colon cancer metabolism and cell fate dynamics.

application_id: 10687561
activity_code: DP2
title: Addressing Cervical Cancer Among People Experiencing Homelessness
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: Public Health Relevance
People experiencing homelessness have far higher rates of cervical cancer and lower screening rates compared to the housed U.S. population. Numerous factors keep this population from accessing cervical cancer screening, leading to worse health outcomes and higher rates of cervical cancer deaths. Leveraging an established multisectoral partnership in Indiana, this study will identify strategies to increase access to cervical cancer screening among people experiencing homelessness, including HPV self-sampling in shelters, to reduce preventable deaths from this disease.

The goal of this competitive renewal application of a 35 year T32 Predoctoral Training Program in Immunology

application_id: 10686592
activity_code: DP2
title: Unraveling the synaptic and circuit mechanisms underlying a plasticity-driving instructive signal
org_name: BRANDEIS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Estimates suggest that more than six million Americans, most of them age 65 and older, may have
Alzheimer’s disease, a brain disorder that affects the hippocampal formation early and is associated with
devastating memory, learning, and other cognitive impairments. Understanding the plasticity
mechanisms underlying the brain’s memory system may prove critical towards achieving rational
progress in finding an effective treatment for the learning deficits observed in Alzheimer’s patients. This
proposal will illuminate the synaptic, cellular, and circuit processes that underlie the entorhinal-
hippocampal circuit’s crucial role in driving learning.

title: Environmental Pathology/Toxicology Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: 8. Project Narrative
Environmental exposure to chemical and physical agents that result in human health impacts continues to be
a major public health problem in the United States and worldwide, and there is a need for well-trained
scientists to conduct research on such pressing health challenges. We believe that to understand how the
environment can affect human heath, environmental health scientists need transdisciplinary training in how to
conduct fundamental research in pathology, toxicology, exposure research, and translational sciences.
However, it is not sufficient to simply learn how to conduct the best cutting edge research, it is also essential
to train our future scientists how to translate their research to solve problems within a context of addressing
health disparities. Emergi...

application_id: 11130927
activity_code: T32
title: Molecular Pathways to Pathogenesis in Toxicology
org_name: NORTH CAROLINA STATE UNIVERSITY RALEIGH
phr: NARRATIVE
Environmental factors are increasingly recognized as having significant contributions to the health of human
populations. Given the breadth of environmental factors and the complexity of this field, it is imperative that we
continue to train the next generation of toxicologists/environmental health science (EHS) researchers with the
technical, operational and professional skills necessary to conduct high impact EHS research, communicate
effectively to a wide variety of audiences, and work as part of multidisciplinary teams to understand how
human health is impacted by environmental factors.
